p1.title: Reviewer scorer does not enforce the published reviewer fixture schema
p1.evidence: reviewer-schema.md requires agent, input_kind, input, expectedVerdict, and expectedFindings (lines 51-59), but isReviewerFixture only checks kind, prompt_template, and expectedVerdict (reviewer-scorer.cjs:46-53). applyCase then silently defaults expectedFindings to [] (lines 101-103).
p1.why_real: Malformed reviewer fixtures can be accepted and counted as passing without the required input or finding checks, which weakens the benchmark substrate and can create false confidence in reviewer prompt promotions.
p1.suggested_fix: Make isReviewerFixture reject missing required fields, or add an explicit schema validation step that fails the fixture/report instead of treating missing expectations as empty.
p1.v2_proof: reviewer-scorer.cjs:46-53 checks only kind/prompt_template/expectedVerdict while reviewer-schema.md §2 (lines 51-59) lists agent/input_kind/input/expectedFindings as required, and applyCase (line 103) defaults expectedFindings to [] — the laxity is real, but the whole path is inert by default (reviewer-scorer.cjs:275-279 exits unless SPECKIT_REVIEWER_BENCHMARKS is enabled) and affects only an opt-in benchmark substrate, not runtime behavior.

p1.title: Reviewer benchmark route writes a non-canonical report path that downstream steps do not read
p1.evidence: The reviewer branch writes `--output .../reviewer-report.json`, but the same workflow later checks `.../report.json` for benchmark completion and promotion input at lines 168, 191, and 195. The confirm workflow has the same reviewer output path at line 187.
p1.why_real: A `--scorer reviewer` run can produce only `reviewer-report.json` while the reducer/journal/promoter path continues to consume `report.json`, so the reviewer benchmark integration can silently skip benchmark_completed evidence and fail synthesis/promotion.
p1.suggested_fix: Either write reviewer results to the canonical `report.json` path or branch every downstream consumer to `state_paths.reviewer_report` when `scoring_method=reviewer`; add an end-to-end reviewer-scorer workflow test.
p1.v2_proof: deep_start-model-benchmark-loop_auto.yaml:165 routes reviewer runs to --output .../reviewer-report.json (reviewer-scorer.cjs:280,300 writes only that path), while lines 168, 191, and 195 unconditionally gate the benchmark_completed journal event, scoringMethod read, and promote-candidate --benchmark-report on .../report.json, which a reviewer run never produces — line 198 shows the authors knew the reviewer path but left these three consumers unbranched.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Branch steps 168/191/195 on scoring_method=reviewer to use state_paths.reviewer_report (or have the reviewer branch also write canonical report.json), mirroring in the confirm YAML.

p1.title: Post-save fingerprint guard validates the wrong file state and normalization
p1.evidence: `validatePostSaveFingerprint()` reads the final target path and computes `computeNormalizedFingerprint()` before comparing at line 1130. The full-auto caller prepares `expectedFingerprint: buildContinuityFingerprint(persistedContent)` in `memory-save.ts:1753-1757`, while the atomic writer writes the pending file and calls `indexPrepared` before `promotePendingFile` (`atomic-index-memory.ts:360-378`).
p1.why_real: Valid full-auto canonical saves can be rejected before promotion because the guard reads the original final file, not the pending persisted content; even after promotion it would compare two different normalizers (`computeNormalizedFingerprint` vs `buildContinuityFingerprint`).
p1.suggested_fix: Validate the pending content before promotion or move this guard after promotion, and use the same fingerprint normalizer on both sides. Add both success and tamper-after-promotion regression tests.
p1.v2_proof: The timing half is real — atomic-index-memory.ts:360-378 writes the pending file then calls indexPrepared (which runs validateCanonicalPreparedSave → POST_SAVE_FINGERPRINT, memory-save.ts:3751/3788) before promotePendingFile, while spec-doc-structure.ts:1105-1130 reads the final target path still holding old content, so content-changing saves would mismatch — but the guard only arms in opt-in full-auto planner mode (postSavePlan gated at memory-save.ts:1754; resolveSavePlannerMode defaults to plan-only per search-flags.vitest.ts:228-229), and the normalizer sub-claim is wrong: memory-save.ts:1023-1031 defines its own buildContinuityFingerprint with normalization identical to computeNormalizedFingerprint (spec-doc-structure.ts:574-580).

p1.title: Secret scrubber covers index artifacts only — the same save request persists the RAW secret to the canonical markdown file on disk, contradicting the shipped 'secrets never reach durable storage' claim
p1.evidence: Non-canonical atomic save: `persistedContent: prepared.qualityLoopResult.passed && fixedContent ? fixedContent : currentContent` (memory-save.ts:3705-3707) — `currentContent` is the raw input, not the scrubbed `prepared.parsed.content`; it is written via writePendingFile (3772-3774) and promoted to the final path (3776-3778). Canonical routed save: `buildCanonicalMergePayload({ ..., content: params.content })` (1664-1670, body built from raw content at 1418-1429) merges raw text into the target
p1.why_real: A memory_save whose content contains e.g. an AWS key increments redaction telemetry and warns 'Redacted N secret(s)... before indexing' while the same request writes the raw key into the promoted spec-folder .md — the canonical durable store that resume flows and future index scans re-read. The cont
p1.suggested_fix: Scrub once at the head of the save path: run scrubSecretsDetailed(params.content) in atomicSaveMemory/buildCanonicalAtomicPreparedSave (fail-closed on SecretScrubberError) and feed the scrubbed string to BOTH parseMemoryContent and persistedContent/merge-payload derivation; add a test asserting the

p1.title: memory_retention_sweep handler drops feedbackRetention options
p1.evidence: handleMemoryRetentionSweep accepts MemoryRetentionSweepArgs, but forwards only { dryRun: args?.dryRun === true } to runMemoryRetentionSweep(). The feedbackRetention sub-object defined by the sweep API is discarded.
p1.why_real: Callers cannot pass shadowEvaluationPassed, runAt, windowMs, extendDays, or test signals through the MCP handler. In active feedback-retention mode this can keep the active gate blocked even when the caller supplied the required pass flag.
p1.suggested_fix: Forward the full validated argument shape, e.g. runMemoryRetentionSweep(database, { dryRun: args?.dryRun === true, feedbackRetention: args?.feedbackRetention }).

p1.title: memory_update provenance metadata bypasses the secret scrubber
p1.evidence: The spec amendment requires scrubbing persisted provenance strings, but memory_update only scrubs title and triggerPhrases at lines 171-179, then persists getInternalProvenanceContext(args) unchanged via persistProvenanceMetadata(database, id, getInternalProvenanceContext(args)) at lines 324-325.
p1.why_real: A secret carried in provenance metadata can still be persisted unredacted, contradicting the phase's all-persisted-fields/provenance-string redaction claim and the fail-closed storage-safety posture.
p1.suggested_fix: Scrub string fields in the provenance context before persistProvenanceMetadata, fail closed on SecretScrubberError, and add a memory_update test covering provenance metadata redaction.

p1.title: Retention sweep revalidates expiry but not protected tier state before deletion
p1.evidence: Expired candidates are selected before the delete transaction, then only delete_after/tombstone state is rechecked via isStillExpired() at lines 470-473. The protected decision at line 516 uses the stale candidate.importanceTier/isPinned values captured before the transaction.
p1.why_real: A concurrent writer can promote an expired row to constitutional/critical or pin it after candidate selection but before deletion. The sweep can still call vectorIndex.deleteMemory() for that now-protected row, violating the phase invariant that protected rows are never deleted solely because TTL ex
p1.suggested_fix: Inside the sweep transaction, re-read importance_tier and is_pinned for each candidate before the protected/delete decision, or make the destructive delete conditional in SQL on the current row still being unprotected.
p1.v2_proof: memory-retention-sweep.ts:471 re-checks only expiry inside the tx while :516 calls isProtectedFromRetentionDelete(candidate) on pre-selection data — the gap is real, but runMemoryRetentionSweep is fully synchronous (better-sqlite3, single warm daemon), so no same-process writer can interleave; only a separate process hitting the same SQLite file in a sub-millisecond window could trigger it, which is not the default single-daemon configuration.
p1.p2_reason: Retention deletion now re-reads current tier and pin metadata inside the transaction.

p1.title: Automatic edge updates are blocked at the degree cap even when no new edge would be added
p1.evidence: The auto edge-bound check runs before the existing-edge lookup at lines 358-369. If countEdgesForNode(sourceId) is already >= MAX_EDGES_PER_NODE, insertEdge returns null before it can discover that the operation is an update of an existing auto/auto-* edge.
p1.why_real: This violates the shipped behavior claim that auto-to-auto updates remain allowed under caps: a saturated node cannot refresh evidence or strength for an existing automatic edge even though the operation does not increase edge count.
p1.suggested_fix: Move the edge-bound check after the existing-row lookup and apply it only when inserting a new automatic edge, not when updating an existing automatic edge.
p1.v2_proof: 002-memory-write-safety/spec.md REQ-003 says 'Allow auto conflict updates ONLY WITHIN existing caps' and checklist CHK-023 tests update 'under cap' — rejecting at/above cap (causal-edges.ts:316-323) is the documented contract, not a violation of it; the code comment at :361-363 says 'under caps' consistently with that.

p1.title: Retention tier protection uses stale candidate metadata after selection
p1.evidence: Expired candidates are selected before the sweep transaction at line 406. Inside the transaction, the code revalidates only delete_after/tombstone state with isStillExpired at line 471, then calls isProtectedFromRetentionDelete(candidate) on the pre-transaction candidate at line 516.
p1.why_real: A concurrent writer can promote or pin a row after selection but before deletion; the delete path can ignore the current protected tier/pin state and still delete it, violating the phase requirement that constitutional/critical/pinned rows are not deleted solely because TTL expired.
p1.suggested_fix: Reload the current tier and pin fields inside the transaction before the delete decision, or make isStillExpired return the current row metadata used by isProtectedFromRetentionDelete.
p1.v2_proof: Same code path as idx 0: memory-retention-sweep.ts:471 vs :516 — real hardening gap under the file's own TOCTOU comment, but unreachable on the default single synchronous daemon; cross-process-only race.
p1.p2_reason: Same stale metadata issue as idx17; fixed by the in-transaction current-row re-read.

p1.title: Changelog 003-001 inflates new test coverage ~5x: claims the five new Vitest files cover '70 tests in total'; they contain 13
p1.evidence: Added bullet: 'Five new Vitest test files: canonical-fingerprint.vitest.ts, memo-storage.vitest.ts, memory-parser-stable-chunks.vitest.ts, incremental-index-foundation.vitest.ts, and vector-index-schema-incremental-foundation.vitest.ts covering 70 tests in total'. Verification table (line 50) repeats 'Focused Vitest suites (9 files, 70 tests) PASS'. Running those five files today yields 13 passing tests (2+4+3+2+2); the landing commit 9e47cb054d itself reports '5 new suites 14/14'. Reconstructin
p1.why_real: A shipped release record materially overstates the phase's delivered test coverage (13-14 actual vs 70 claimed, attributed explicitly to the five new files). This is exactly the verification-honesty surface the epic's release docs are supposed to guarantee; downstream phases and reviewers cite these
p1.suggested_fix: Correct the Added bullet to the real per-file count (13 tests across the five new files) and restate the verification row as the actual focused-run composition (file list + true total), matching the commit-message numbers.
p1.v2_proof: changelog-003-001-incremental-index-foundation.md Added bullet now reads 'contributing 13 focused tests; the verification run below exercises the wider 9-file suite at 70 tests' — matching the actual count (2+4+3+2+2=13 it() blocks, no it.each, verified across the five files); the inflated attribution the seat quoted is no longer in the file.

p1.title: Low-level memory delete can commit after causal tombstone sweep failure
p1.evidence: deleteAncillaryMemoryRows calls sweepCausalEdges at lines 138-145 but catches and ignores all errors at lines 149-151; delete_memory_from_database then continues to DELETE FROM memory_index at line 692.
p1.why_real: If tombstone insertion or causal edge deletion fails, the memory row can still be removed, leaving active/orphan causal edges or losing the promised tombstone audit trail. A missing causal_edges table already returns an empty sweep result, so swallowing all sweep failures is broader than legacy comp
p1.suggested_fix: Only tolerate known legacy absence cases, and rethrow sweep/tombstone failures so the surrounding transaction rolls back instead of deleting the memory row.

p1.title: Direct tombstone sweeps do not bump the causal-edge generation cache key
p1.evidence: `sweepCausalEdges()` only clears degree/graph-signal caches after deletes (`sweep.ts:274-275`). The generation counter used by `memory_search` cache keys is bumped only inside `causal-edges.ts` `invalidateDegreeCache()` (`causal-edges.ts:221-225`), while production paths such as vector-index mutation cleanup call `sweepCausalEdges()` directly (`vector-index-mutations.ts:138-145`).
p1.why_real: Deletes routed through direct sweep callers can change the causal graph without changing `getCausalEdgesGeneration()`, so causal-boosted `memory_search` cache entries can survive a causal-edge delete. This violates the cache-invalidation invariant documented in `causal-edges.ts` and affects shipped
p1.suggested_fix: Move causal generation bumping into the shared sweep/invalidation path, or route all production deletes through a wrapper that both tombstones and bumps the generation. Add a regression that direct `sweepCausalEdges()` changes `getCausalEdgesGeneration()`.

p1.title: Governed drift traversal returns out-of-scope edge IDs and relations
p1.evidence: handleMemoryDriftWhy builds incoming/outgoing chains before scope authorization, only checks the source row at lines 594-600, only filters related memory details at lines 637-645, then returns incoming/outgoing/allEdges at lines 741-753 unchanged.
p1.why_real: A scoped caller authorized for the source memory can still receive edge ids, endpoint ids, directions, relations, and strengths for out-of-scope neighboring memories. The traversal also touches edge access timestamps before the fail-closed source check via causalEdges.getCausalChain.
p1.suggested_fix: Authorize the source memory before any traversal, and filter or refuse edges whose source or target rows do not match the supplied tenant/user/agent scope before constructing the response.
p1.v2_proof: handlers/causal-graph.ts:594-611 fail-closes only the source memory and 636-645 filters only relatedMemories details, while 744-746 returns incoming/outgoing/allEdges with out-of-scope endpoint IDs/relations/strengths — but scope is opt-in per-call (lines 507-510), no memory content leaks, and this exact shape was a deliberate remediation design (commit c0da106b44), making the residual edge-metadata exposure a hardening item, not a default-config P1.
p1.p2_reason: Governed traversal filtering is security-sensitive and too broad for a minor P2 seat.

p1.title: Correction undo can mark a correction undone while its causal edge remains active
p1.evidence: undo_correction marks memory_corrections.is_undone=1 at lines 604-610, then attempts sweepCausalEdges at lines 620-637, but catches edge cleanup failures at lines 672-675 and still commits the transaction.
p1.why_real: A tombstone/delete failure leaves the correction-owned causal edge active while the correction is recorded as undone, so graph state contradicts correction state and future audits cannot trust undo semantics.
p1.suggested_fix: Run owned edge cleanup in the same transaction as a required step and rethrow failures, or move the is_undone update after successful edge cleanup.
p1.v2_proof: corrections.ts:604-610 sets is_undone=1 before the sweep, and the catch at 672-675 swallows sweep failures so the transaction commits with the owned edge still active — accurately described, but it mirrors the deliberately best-effort creation side ('Causal edge creation is optional', line 518), is warn-logged, and requires the tombstone sweep itself to throw, so it is an accepted fail-open inconsistency window, not a P1.
p1.p2_reason: Changing correction undo transaction semantics is behavior-risky beyond P2 polish.

p1.title: MemoStore cycle checks rely on a stale per-instance edge count
p1.evidence: MemoStore snapshots dependencyEdgeCount once at construction at lines 102-115, skips cycle checks when that cached count is 0 at line 210, and returns no dependents from collectDependents when the cached count is 0 at lines 217-223.
p1.why_real: Two MemoStore instances against the same database can diverge: an older instance constructed at zero edges will skip cycle detection and dependency invalidation even after another instance inserts edges. That breaks the claimed dependency DAG and can miss invalidations or allow cycles.
p1.suggested_fix: Remove the cached count shortcut, refresh the count before cycle/invalidation checks, or enforce a single writer through the storage API.
p1.v2_proof: memo.ts:103-104 explicitly documents the single-writer invariant, and the only production instantiation is a short-lived per-call store inside planMemoizedIndexing (incremental-index.ts:741) that never calls addDependencyEdge — addDependencyEdge has zero non-test callers — and better-sqlite3's synchronous execution leaves no window for another writer during that store's lifetime, so the divergence scenario cannot occur today.

p1.title: Completed incremental-index spec still claims handler integration that is not shipped
p1.evidence: The completed spec keeps `memory_index_scan` handler integration in scope (`Update handlers/memory-index.ts so scan planning consults memo state before parsing and embedding`) and marks status Complete, but the shipped handler only calls `incrementalIndex.categorizeFilesForIndexing(files)` at `handlers/memory-index.ts:823-843`. `planMemoizedIndexing(` appears only in `lib/storage/incremental-index.ts:718` and tests, not in the handler. The implementation summary also admits handler scan behavior
p1.why_real: This is a materially false completion claim for REQ-001/REQ-008/SC-001 style behavior: runtime scans cannot report or use memo hits, chunk hits, or dependency-invalidated paths, so unchanged chunks are not skipped via the new memo DAG.
p1.suggested_fix: Either amend spec/checklist status to mark handler integration deferred, or wire `memory_index_scan` to extract stable chunks, populate chunk metadata, upsert memo records, call `planMemoizedIndexing()`, and report memo/chunk/DAG telemetry.
p1.v2_proof: Accurate that handlers/memory-index.ts:826 only calls categorizeFilesForIndexing and planMemoizedIndexing is unwired, but the deferral is a documented parent planning amendment — implementation-summary.md:59/103/116/146 and the changelog Follow-Ups state 'handler scan behavior intentionally unchanged' and checklist line 98 records it as intentional — leaving only a stale In-Scope bullet at spec.md:120 to amend, not a materially false completion claim.
p1.p2_reason: Spec edits are forbidden and wiring handler integration is larger than a minor safe fix.

p1.title: Memo DAG invalidation and cycle checks are only correct for a single MemoStore writer
p1.evidence: `dependencyEdgeCount` is counted once and the comment says it is correct only while this single `MemoStore` is the sole writer (`memo.ts:102-105`). `addDependencyEdge()` skips cycle detection when that cached count is 0 (`memo.ts:210`), and `collectDependents()` returns no dependents when the cached count is 0 (`memo.ts:217-223`).
p1.why_real: A store constructed against an empty DB can miss edges inserted by another store later, allowing cycles and missed invalidation. That contradicts the shipped claim that `memo.ts` provides dependency traversal, invalidation, and cycle rejection as durable SQLite primitives.
p1.suggested_fix: Do not cache dependency-edge presence for correctness-sensitive operations. Refresh the count from SQLite or always run graph traversal for cycle detection and dependent collection.
p1.v2_proof: Duplicate of idx 3: memo.ts:103-104 documents the single-writer constraint, no production code calls addDependencyEdge (tests only), and the sole production store (incremental-index.ts:741) is constructed, used synchronously, and discarded inside one call, so no second-writer scenario exists in shipped code.

p1.title: record_correction bypasses insertEdge validation and generation counter
p1.evidence: Lines 505-514: raw INSERT INTO causal_edges with hardcoded strength=1.0, no call to insertEdge(). The standard insertEdge (causal-edges.ts:292) enforces MAX_AUTO_STRENGTH=0.5 for auto edges, enforceRelationWindowCap, MAX_EDGES_PER_NODE, self-loop rejection, and contradiction detection. record_correction bypasses all of these. Additionally, record_correction calls invalidateGraphCaches (line 515) which calls clearDegreeCacheForDb + clearGraphSignalsCache, but does NOT call bumpCausalEdgesGenerati
p1.why_real: Correction-created causal edges (strength 1.0) exceed the MAX_AUTO_STRENGTH cap (0.5) that all other auto-created edges obey. The missing generation bump means consumers comparing generation counters see a stale causal graph. This is a real correctness gap: if a user runs supersede_memory then immed
p1.suggested_fix: Use the existing insertEdge() function instead of raw SQL for the correction causal edge, or at minimum call invalidateDegreeCache() (which bumps the generation) instead of invalidateGraphCaches(). Also pass a createdBy like 'auto-correction' so the auto-strength cap applies consistently, or use cre
p1.v2_proof: corrections.ts:505-514 raw-INSERTs the causal edge then calls the module-local invalidateGraphCaches (corrections.ts:191-203) which omits bumpCausalEdgesGeneration — unlike sweep.ts:216 and causal-edges.ts invalidateDegreeCache:215 — while memory-search.ts:952-953 folds that generation into the search cache key under default-on SPECKIT_CAUSAL_BOOST, so cached causal-boosted searches stay stale after a correction creates its edge (the strength-1.0 sub-claim is weaker since corrections are user-initiated, manual-class).
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Make corrections.ts's invalidateGraphCaches call bumpCausalEdgesGeneration first (matching sweep.ts:212-227), or route the correction edge through insertEdge with a correction createdBy.

p1.title: deleteEdgesByIds deletes edges one row at a time inside sweep transaction
p1.evidence: Lines 201-208: deleteEdgesByIds iterates edgeIds and executes DELETE FROM causal_edges WHERE id = ? individually for each edge. This is called from sweepCausalEdges (line 264) which is in turn called from deleteEdgesForMemory (causal-edges.ts:870), which is called per-memory inside bulk delete loops (memory-crud-delete.ts:250, memory-bulk-delete.ts:268). For a bulk delete of N memories with M edges each, the inner loop performs N*M individual DELETE statements instead of batching.
p1.why_real: During bulk deletes (memory_bulk_delete or memory_delete with specFolder), each memory's edges are swept individually via deleteEdgesForMemory. With large causal graphs, this produces O(N*M) individual DELETE statements inside a single transaction. SQLite better-sqlite3 prepares and executes each st
p1.suggested_fix: Batch the DELETE using WHERE id IN (?, ?, ...) with chunked placeholders (the codebase already has this pattern in collectPrunedCheckpointSnapshotPaths). Similarly, batch the tombstone INSERTs. Or move sweepCausalEdges calls outside the per-memory loop and pass all edgeIds at once.
p1.v2_proof: sweep.ts:204 prepares the DELETE statement once outside the loop and reuses it for every id inside the single sweep transaction (runInTransaction at 234), so the 'prepares and executes each statement' premise is false and per-primary-key deletes inside one better-sqlite3 transaction are a micro-cost perf nit with no correctness impact and no measurement showing a problem.

p1.title: Shadow stats logged via console.info write to stdout, polluting the stdio MCP JSON-RPC channel when the feature is enabled
p1.evidence: console.info('[memory_match_triggers] Semantic trigger shadow', semanticTriggerShadow) — console.info writes to stdout in Node. The server runs StdioServerTransport (context-server.ts:2179, transport created at startup), where stdout carries JSON-RPC frames. No console patching exists anywhere in the server (verified by grep). Repo convention is unambiguous: context-server.ts and all handlers use console.error/console.warn (stderr) even for purely informational startup logs. The phase's own test
p1.why_real: Shadow mode's entire purpose is to be enabled in production to gather promotion evidence. The moment SPECKIT_SEMANTIC_TRIGGERS=true, every memory_match_triggers call without a strong lexical match injects a non-JSON, multi-line util.inspect blob into the protocol stream — corrupting framing for stri
p1.suggested_fix: Change console.info to console.error (or the structured stderr logger used elsewhere) so shadow telemetry goes to stderr like every other server log.

p1.title: Empty trigger responses drop all semantic-trigger telemetry
p1.evidence: The handler computes semantic shadow/union state before the empty-result branch, but lines 585-608 return createMCPEmptyResponse without extraMeta. The only response path that attaches semanticTriggerShadow/semanticTriggerUnion is the later success response at lines 783-787.
p1.why_real: The feature is specifically meant to evaluate lexical-empty paraphrases. In shadow mode those requests still have results.length === 0, so their semanticCount/topScore/no_query_embedding/no_trigger_embeddings telemetry is silently discarded and cannot support the claimed shadow evaluation/promotion
p1.suggested_fix: Include the same semanticTriggerShadow/semanticTriggerUnion extraMeta on the empty-response path before returning.
p1.p2_reason: Duplicate of idx 6; fixed by preserving semantic telemetry on empty trigger responses.

p1.title: Scoped semantic matching filters too late
p1.evidence: Union mode calls `matchSemanticTriggers(queryEmbedding, semanticCache, { max: limit })` before `filterSemanticMatchesByScope(..., args)` at lines 526-530. Shadow mode calls `computeSemanticTriggerShadow(...)` at lines 561-568 without passing `specFolder`, `tenantId`, `userId`, or `agentId`.
p1.why_real: Out-of-scope memories can affect scoped callers: shadow metadata can count cross-scope semantic matches, and union mode can let out-of-scope high scores consume max slots or trip the global margin gate before scoped filtering runs.
p1.suggested_fix: Apply scope before semantic ranking/margin/max, or load a scoped semantic cache. Pass the same scope into shadow stats and add tenant/spec-folder tests where out-of-scope rows outrank in-scope rows.
p1.v2_proof: memory-triggers.ts:526-530 applies filterSemanticMatchesByScope only AFTER matchSemanticTriggers has run its margin gate and max slice (semantic-trigger-matcher.ts:433-442), and the shadow call at memory-triggers.ts:561-568 passes no scope args — both real, but the entire path is double-gated default-off (SPECKIT_SEMANTIC_TRIGGERS opt-in at semantic-trigger-matcher.ts:103-105, union additionally requires SPECKIT_SEMANTIC_TRIGGERS_MODE=union at memory-triggers.ts:182-186, and trigger embeddings only exist if the default-off backfill flag ran, trigger-embedding-backfill.ts:52-54), so no default-config runtime effect today.
p1.p2_reason: Scope-before-ranking requires scoped semantic cache/ranking changes and new fixtures; too broad for P2.

p1.title: Semantic trigger corpus is broader than lexical corpus
p1.evidence: Backfill seeds `memory_trigger_embeddings` from every `memory_index` row with non-empty `trigger_phrases` at lines 154-160. The semantic loader later joins ready rows without document-type, top-level-anchor, or memory embedding-status filters in `semantic-trigger-matcher.ts` lines 272-296.
p1.why_real: Shadow metrics and union hits can be produced for rows lexical trigger matching would not consider, such as non-canonical memory rows, chunk/anchored rows, or pending/failed records. That makes shadow evidence non-comparable and can surface adjacent-path records when union is enabled.
p1.suggested_fix: Reuse the lexical trigger-source predicate for backfill and semantic cache loading. Add regression fixtures for non-canonical `document_type`, non-empty `anchor_id`, and pending `embedding_status` rows.
p1.v2_proof: Backfill seeds from ALL memory_index rows with trigger_phrases (trigger-embedding-backfill.ts:154-161) and the semantic loader joins with no document_type/anchor_id/embedding_status predicates (semantic-trigger-matcher.ts:272-296), while the lexical corpus is restricted to embedding_status='success' canonical doc types with no anchor or the continuity anchor (lib/parsing/trigger-matcher.ts:209-219) — a real corpus mismatch, but inert on default config because both the backfill flag (trigger-embedding-backfill.ts:134-137, 'skipped_default_off') and SPECKIT_SEMANTIC_TRIGGERS are default-off.
p1.p2_reason: Changing semantic corpus predicates may alter retrieval behavior and needs broader regression coverage.

p1.title: Changelog 005-004 'Files Changed' omits memory-retention-sweep.ts — the phase's only live-mutation surface
p1.evidence: Commit f68fb3c98b (feat(027/005/004)) shows `lib/governance/memory-retention-sweep.ts | 165 +++` — the new feedbackRetention branch including the active-mode UPDATE/DELETE apply path (memory-retention-sweep.ts:260-512). The changelog's Files Changed table lists only feedback-retention-reducer.ts, edge-tier-basement.ts, two test files, and ENV_REFERENCE.md. The 004 implementation-summary mentions 'a default-off retention sweep branch' in prose but never names the file either.
p1.why_real: The sweep modification is where retention rows are actually mutated (extend/protect/delete in active mode), i.e. the highest-risk surface this phase shipped. Every sibling changelog (001, 003, 005) enumerates all production files of its commit; only 004 omits one. Anyone auditing the phase from its
p1.suggested_fix: Add a `lib/governance/memory-retention-sweep.ts` row to the 004 changelog Files Changed table (and name the file in implementation-summary 'What Was Built').
p1.v2_proof: changelog-005-004-feedback-retention-reducer.md:53 now lists lib/governance/memory-retention-sweep.ts ('the phase's mutation site') in Files Changed — the row was added by remediation commit ba3dddad1d (docs(027): epic deep-review remediation round 1).

p1.title: Duplicate source dedupe can defeat same-query causal preference
p1.evidence: The reducer dedupes by memory_id by keeping the newest event at lines 113-116, then applies same-query preference only to that retained event at lines 121-126.
p1.why_real: If a source was shown in the cited query and then shown again in a different query before the citation, the same-query evidence is discarded before ranking. Under the five-source cap, this can select and insert weaker causal edges while omitting a same-query source, contradicting the reducer's deter
p1.suggested_fix: Include same-query status in the per-memory dedupe comparator, or sort candidate events by same-query preference before deduping each source.
p1.p2_reason: Real same-query preference bug; included same-query status in source dedupe comparator with test coverage.

p1.title: Retention dry-run previews ignore ledger feedback unless tests inject signals
p1.evidence: buildFeedbackRetentionReport resolves signals as args.signals ?? (dryRun ? [] : aggregateEvents(...)), so dry-run mode never reads feedback events unless an internal caller passes signals. The reviewed dry-run test covers only the injected-signals path.
p1.why_real: With SPECKIT_FEEDBACK_RETENTION_LEARNING enabled, an operator dry-run can report delete decisions for important memories that the non-dry-run path would extend from ledger feedback, undermining the safety preview before active retention.
p1.suggested_fix: Provide a read-only aggregation path for dry-run, or expose/populate signals for the dry-run tool path, then add a dry-run test with feedback events and no injected signals.
p1.v2_proof: memory-retention-sweep.ts:293-295 confirms signals = args.signals ?? (dryRun ? [] : aggregateEvents(...)), so an operator dry-run omits ledger feedback — real fidelity gap, but the branch only exists under default-off SPECKIT_FEEDBACK_RETENTION_LEARNING and the skip is consistent with the phase's documented zero-DB-writes dryRun contract (aggregateEvents calls initBatchLearning DDL, batch-learning.ts:262), making it a preview-fidelity improvement, not a live correctness defect.

p1.title: Parent control file still claims all three shipped phases are 'Spec-scaffolded' with 0% completion
p1.evidence: Phase map rows 109-111 list 001/002/003 as 'Spec-scaffolded' and the frontmatter continuity (lines 17-18, 26, 29) says recent_action='Scaffolded 009 phase-parent', next_safe_action='Plan/implement phase 001 (typed I/O adapter) first', completion_pct: 0, fingerprint sha256:000...0. Meanwhile all three child packets record shipped state (001 graph-metadata derived.status='implemented', 002 'implemented_with_scope_exceptions', 003 'complete', each implementation-summary completion_pct: 100) and all
p1.why_real: This is a materially false shipped claim in the phase's only required authored parent doc, and it directly violates the repo's own completion-reconciliation rule (packet docs must not claim conflicting completion states). The stale next_safe_action affirmatively instructs a resuming agent to plan/im
p1.suggested_fix: Refresh the parent spec.md phase-map Status column (implemented / implemented-with-scope-exceptions / complete), update the _memory.continuity block (recent_action, next_safe_action, completion_pct, real fingerprint) via the canonical save path, and backfill derived.last_active_child_id in the paren
p1.v2_proof: 006-gem-team-adoption/spec.md:109-111 now lists all three phases as 'Complete' and spec.md:29 has completion_pct: 100 (fixed in remediation commit ba3dddad1d); parent graph-metadata.json derived status is 'completed' with all 3 children_ids present — only cosmetic residue remains (spec.md:17-18 recent_action/next_safe_action strings and the all-zeros fingerprint), and phase-parent resume follows graph-metadata.json, not the spec continuity block.

p1.title: Claude/Codex dispatch templates point at OpenCode agent definitions
p1.evidence: Claude path convention says use .claude/agents at line 19 and the consistency guard requires .claude/agents/code.md for @code at line 162, but the mandatory task format says Agent Definition: [.opencode/agents/<name>.md]. Codex has the same contradiction at .codex/agents/orchestrate.toml:24, :167, and :200.
p1.why_real: Following the mandatory task template in Claude or Codex can load the wrong runtime mirror or fail the prompt/agent consistency guard before dispatch, changing delegated-agent behavior.
p1.suggested_fix: Parameterize the task format and PDR examples per runtime so Claude uses .claude/agents/*.md and Codex uses .codex/agents/*.toml.

p1.title: Canonical save lock can expire during a live save
p1.evidence: CANONICAL_SAVE_STALE_MS is 30000. acquireCanonicalSaveLock removes any lock whose directory mtime is older than that at lines 415-420, with no heartbeat or PID liveness check.
p1.why_real: A legitimate save that runs or stalls longer than 30 seconds can be treated as stale, letting another save remove its lock and run concurrently against the same canonical files.
p1.suggested_fix: Use a heartbeat or PID-liveness based stale check, extend the timeout, and fail closed when the owner cannot be proven dead.

p1.title: Concurrent child saves can drop phase-parent children_ids
p1.evidence: updatePhaseParentPointer builds children_ids from one read snapshot and then atomicWriteJson writes it. The only save lock is acquired for lockedSpecFolder at line 786, but child saves update the shared parent graph metadata via updatePhaseParentPointersAfterSave at line 803.
p1.why_real: Two child saves under the same phase parent can both read the same parent children_ids, append different child ids, and last-writer-wins will erase the other child. That corrupts phase-parent graph metadata used for traversal/resume.
p1.suggested_fix: Lock the phase parent graph-metadata.json update with a parent-scoped lock, or re-read/retry the children_ids union immediately before rename under a shared parent lock.
p1.v2_proof: generate-context.ts:852-853 locks only the saved child folder while updatePhaseParentPointer (generate-context.ts:532 read → :558 atomicWriteJson) does an unlocked read-modify-write on the shared parent graph-metadata.json — the lost-update race is real (the safety comment at :546-547 only covers same-child idempotency), but the window is milliseconds, children_ids are pre-populated at scaffold time, the append self-heals on the dropped child's next save, and last_active pointer last-writer-wins is acceptable, so it is not a today-default P1 integrity break.
p1.p2_reason: Parent graph locking changes affect concurrency semantics and are too risky for P2.

p1.title: Mirror component-authoring gates miss mirror agent paths
p1.evidence: Claude @code blocks component authoring only for .opencode/skills, .opencode/agents, and .opencode/commands. Codex repeats this at .codex/agents/code.toml:23, while their orchestrators use the same .opencode-only trigger at .claude/agents/orchestrate.md:411 and .codex/agents/orchestrate.toml:416.
p1.why_real: A task that edits .claude/agents/** or .codex/agents/** can bypass both the orchestrator routing gate and the @code receiver gate, allowing @code to author agent component docs instead of routing through the documentation lane.
p1.suggested_fix: Include runtime mirror component paths in the trigger and receiver gates, at minimum .claude/agents/** and .codex/agents/**, or define a shared component-path predicate used by all mirrors.
p1.v2_proof: .claude/agents/code.md:19,359,491 and .codex/agents/code.toml:23 plus orchestrate.md:411/orchestrate.toml:416 do enumerate only .opencode/** trigger paths, but the gate's categorical first sentence ('@code MUST NOT author skill, agent, or command components') at code.md:19 is path-independent and covers mirror files, the repo convention edits the .opencode canonical first with mirrors synced, and this is governance prose, not runtime code — an enumeration-completeness gap, not a behavior defect.
p1.p2_reason: Mirror component-authoring gate expansion is cross-agent policy work, not a small local fix.

p1.title: Wave 2 spec marks deferred gate surfaces as required/completed
p1.evidence: REQ-005 says sk-code/SKILL.md requires the boundary contract-first gate, and REQ-009 says the debug-delegation template/scaffold carry typed fields. Current committed grep found no change_class, Boundary contract-first, or contract-first anchors in .opencode/skills/sk-code/SKILL.md; scaffold-debug-delegation.sh has no root_cause/target_files/fix_recommendations/AGENT_IO_HANDOFF anchors. The same spec only later notes these edits were out of approved scope at line 138.
p1.why_real: The shipped docs still present P0/P1 acceptance criteria as implemented even though those runtime/generator surfaces do not contain the behavior. Consumers using sk-code or generated debug handoffs will not get the claimed gate/schema.
p1.suggested_fix: Either implement those surfaces in a scope-approved pass, or amend spec/checklist/tasks so REQ-005 and REQ-009 are explicit deferred scope exceptions rather than completed requirements.
p1.v2_proof: 002-scoped-preexec-and-handoff-gates/spec.md:63 sets Status 'Implemented with scope exceptions', spec.md:138 explicitly states the sk-code/template/scaffold edits were 'read and flagged, not modified', and tasks.md:56-82 (T003/T004/T009/T012/T015) plus checklist.md:77-111 (CHK-024/026/042) all record those REQ-005/REQ-009 surfaces as out of approved write scope — a documented deferral, consistently reconciled across every completion surface, not a false completion claim.

p1.title: Phase-parent graph updates are not protected by a shared parent lock
p1.evidence: main() acquires the canonical-save lock for only the saved spec folder at lines 785-786, then child saves update the direct phase parent's graph-metadata.json via updatePhaseParentPointersAfterSave at lines 505-512 using a read/modify/rename write at lines 465-491.
p1.why_real: Two concurrent saves for sibling phase children hold different child locks while mutating the same parent graph-metadata.json, so the last writer can drop the other child's children_ids update and make resume/graph metadata nondeterministic.
p1.suggested_fix: Lock the phase parent, or a dedicated parent graph-metadata lock, before updating parent metadata and merge/retry from the latest file while holding that shared lock.
p1.v2_proof: Duplicate of idx 1: generate-context.ts:852-853 (child-scoped lock) vs :532/:558 unlocked parent read-modify-write in updatePhaseParentPointer via updatePhaseParentPointersAfterSave (:561-580) — same real but narrow, self-healing lost-update race on parent graph-metadata.json.
p1.p2_reason: Duplicate parent graph locking concern; too risky for P2.

p1.title: memory_update idempotency key never varies per logical update — second distinct update is a permanent idempotency_key_conflict when SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY=true
p1.evidence: requestFingerprint is only { id, contentHash } (memory-crud-update.ts:225-228); deriveIdempotencyReceiptKey builds the receipt key from operation+contentHash+fingerprintHash and deliberately excludes the payload (idempotency-receipts.ts:87-102). update_memory never writes content_hash (vector-index-mutations.ts:490-538), receipts have no TTL and nothing in the codebase deletes from memory_idempotency_receipts (vector-index-schema.ts:658-670; repo-wide grep). So after one successful flag-on updat
p1.why_real: Unlike memory_save (where new content yields a new key, and where 023's follow-up explicitly acknowledges the force-retry-conflict + TTL gap), the update-path defect is acknowledged nowhere in 007 or 023 and structurally cannot be cured by a TTL alone — within any receipt lifetime, all legitimate me
p1.suggested_fix: Include the normalized effective payload hash (or the guarded update fields) in the memory_update receipt-key material so a fresh logical update derives a fresh key, reserving 'conflict' for genuinely ambiguous same-key retries; alternatively key on a per-request fingerprint (and add receipt TTL as
p1.p2_reason: memory_update idempotency fingerprint now varies by effective logical mutation.

p1.title: memory_update idempotency key blocks legitimate later metadata updates
p1.evidence: The memory_update receipt key fingerprint is only {id, contentHash} at lines 221-228, while the changed metadata fields are only in payload at lines 229-237. After the first successful update stores a receipt, any later update to the same memory whose content_hash has not changed derives the same receipt key but a different payload_hash, so lines 243-253 return idempotency_key_conflict before writing.
p1.why_real: memory_update changes title/triggerPhrases/importance metadata without changing content_hash, so SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY=true makes normal subsequent metadata edits look like changed-payload retries and fail closed.
p1.suggested_fix: Derive update receipt keys from a logical mutation fingerprint that distinguishes fresh metadata updates, or expire/scope receipts so only true retries share a key.
p1.p2_reason: Same memory_update idempotency key issue as idx22; later metadata updates get fresh keys.

p1.title: Receipt replay never sets the promised replayed marker
p1.evidence: lookupIdempotencyReceiptByKey returns JSON.parse(row.response_payload) unchanged at lines 139-143. The replay callers return that response directly in memory-save.ts lines 3465-3466 and memory-crud-update.ts lines 240-241, bypassing response-builder.ts lines 699-700 where replayed:true would be emitted only from an IndexResult.
p1.why_real: The changelog claims identical retries replay with replayed:true, but clients receive the original response with no replay marker and cannot distinguish a replay from the first write.
p1.suggested_fix: Wrap parsed stored responses on replay and add replayed:true to the MCP envelope data before returning.

p1.title: Concurrent conflicting updates can commit before receipt conflict detection
p1.evidence: The receipt lookup runs before mutation at lines 219-258, title updates can await embedding at lines 270-275, the DB mutation commits in lines 315-459, and the receipt is stored only afterward at lines 542-548. storeIdempotencyReceipt uses ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, and the update handler ignores its boolean result.
p1.why_real: Two concurrent updates with the same derived key but different payloads can both miss before either receipt exists, both mutate the row, and only one receipt is kept. That violates the changed-payload fail-closed invariant and can leave the stored receipt describing a response that no longer matches
p1.suggested_fix: Reserve the receipt key transactionally before the mutation, or perform lookup, conflict check, mutation, and receipt store under one SQLite write transaction with a handled unique conflict path.
p1.v2_proof: Race structure is real (lookup at memory-crud-update.ts:222-241, await embedding at :275 yields the event loop, receipt stored at :544 with the ON-CONFLICT-DO-NOTHING boolean from idempotency-receipts.ts:163-175 ignored), but isMemoryIdempotencyEnabled (idempotency-receipts.ts:54-57) requires SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY to be explicitly set — the whole path is default-off, so no default-configuration impact.
p1.p2_reason: Transactional receipt reservation is a concurrency redesign, not a low-risk P2 fix.

p1.title: memory_save follow-up arguments are rejected by the strict public schema
p1.evidence: memorySaveSchema at lines 242-261 allows routeAs and mergeModeHint but omits plannerMode and targetAnchorId; ALLOWED_PARAMETERS repeats the same omission at line 610. memory-save.ts emits follow-up args containing plannerMode:'full-auto' and targetAnchorId at lines 1840-1845, and the handler consumes those fields at lines 3046 and 3051.
p1.why_real: With strict schemas enabled by default, the tool can return an apply follow-up that the same MCP validation layer rejects as unknown parameters, breaking the canonical routed-save workflow.
p1.suggested_fix: Add plannerMode and targetAnchorId to memorySaveSchema and ALLOWED_PARAMETERS, with the same enum/string validation used by the handler.
p1.v2_proof: memorySaveSchema (tool-input-schemas.ts:242-261) and ALLOWED_PARAMETERS (:610) omit plannerMode/targetAnchorId; getSchema is strict by default (:29, SPECKIT_STRICT_SCHEMAS unset → .strict()); context-server.ts:1057-1058 validates every registered tool; memory-save.ts:1841-1846 emits the apply follow-up with plannerMode:'full-auto' (+targetAnchorId) and the handler consumes them at :3047/:3052; planner default is plan-only (search-flags.ts:126-134), so a schema-valid routed save (routeAs/mergeModeHint) returns follow-up args the same validation layer then rejects as unknown keys.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Add plannerMode (enum plan-only|hybrid|full-auto) and targetAnchorId (string) to memorySaveSchema and ALLOWED_PARAMETERS['memory_save'].

p1.title: Idempotency replays do not set the documented replayed:true marker
p1.evidence: The replay branch returns lookup.response directly; memory_update does the same at memory-crud-update.ts:240-241. lookupIdempotencyReceiptByKey just parses row.response_payload in idempotency-receipts.ts:139-143, and tests assert the replay equals the original response in tests/memory-idempotency-and-near-duplicate.vitest.ts:93-97. This contradicts 002 implementation-summary.md:60 and changelog-007-002:23, which claim identical retries replay with replayed:true.
p1.why_real: Clients cannot distinguish a replay from the original write even though the shipped docs advertise that signal.
p1.suggested_fix: Inject data.replayed = true, or equivalent, when returning replayed MCP responses for both memory_save and memory_update, and add handler-level tests for the marker.
p1.v2_proof: 007/002 implementation-summary.md:60,64,87 now document that 'phase 023 later removed the marker so replay returns the original response verbatim' — the verbatim replay (memory-save.ts:3466-3468, memory-crud-update.ts:240-241) is the documented contract, and no changelog still advertises replayed:true (only unrelated MCP-proxy replay text in v3.5.0.4.md).

p1.title: Critical and pinned rows are documented as protected but are not guarded
p1.evidence: isProtectedExistingRow protects null/human source, constitutional tier, or constitutional paths only at lines 88-91. It does not check importance_tier === 'critical' or is_pinned, despite 001 implementation-summary.md:104 claiming the protected set includes constitutional or critical rows plus pinned data.
p1.why_real: An automated provenance context can still update protected fields on a system/agent row that is critical or pinned but not constitutional, so the documented protection is not enforced.
p1.suggested_fix: Either add critical and pinned checks to isProtectedExistingRow with tests, or correct the implementation summary to the narrower actual contract.
p1.v2_proof: memory-crud-update.ts:88-91 protects only null/human source_kind, constitutional tier, or constitutional path — no importance_tier==='critical' and no is_pinned check anywhere in the file — while 007/001 implementation-summary.md Key Decisions row states the protected set is 'rows with importance_tier constitutional or critical, plus pinned data', so an automated caller can today retier/retitle a critical or pinned agent/system row.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Add critical-tier and is_pinned checks to isProtectedExistingRow with regression tests, or amend the 001 decision row to the narrower shipped contract.

p1.title: Phase parent still reports the epic as plan-only and planned
p1.evidence: The parent metadata says the status is draft/plan-only/not implemented, and the phase map still lists phases as Planned at lines 121-125. The reviewed changelogs and child implementation summaries mark shipped leaves as implemented/completed, and the committed code contains the claimed changes.
p1.why_real: The parent is the control document for resume and phase status, so the current committed docs mislead reviewers/operators about shipped state.
p1.suggested_fix: Update the parent status and phase map to reflect completed/implemented children, or clearly mark the parent map as historical rather than authoritative.
p1.v2_proof: 007 parent spec.md:60 Status 'Draft (plan only — not implemented)', phase map rows all 'Planned' (:121-125), continuity completion_pct 0, and graph-metadata.json:42 status 'draft_(plan_only_—_not_implemented)' with last_active_child_id null — while child 002 implementation-summary.md:43 says 'Implemented', child 005 says 'Completed', and the claimed code (provenance guard, idempotency receipts, retention protection) is verifiably in current source.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Update the parent status, phase-map rows, continuity block, and graph-metadata derived status to reflect the implemented children.

p1.title: deriveSourceKindFromContext defaults to 'human' for all MCP tool writes lacking explicit provenance
p1.evidence: sourceKindFromLabel (line 47-68) tests tool names against regex patterns for 'human|manual|user|operator|author', 'feedback|validate|validation', 'import|ingest|scan|index', and 'agent|assistant|claude|codex|opencode|automation|bot'. Neither 'memory_save' nor 'memory_update' match any of these patterns. The fallback at line 83-93 checks scope: if userId is present it returns 'human' (line 93). MCP client calls always carry a userId in scope, so every direct memory_save/memory_update is classifie
p1.why_real: The changelog claims 'Every new record now carries a server-derived source_kind' and 'Callers cannot assert source_kind directly.' The derivation is server-side but its default path misclassifies any automated caller that doesn't inject explicit provenanceSource/provenanceActor. The indexSingleFile
p1.suggested_fix: Add 'memory_save' and 'memory_update' to the agent regex in sourceKindFromLabel, or change the fallback from 'human' to 'system' when the tool field is present but unmatched.
p1.v2_proof: write-provenance.ts:84-94 plus the 007/001 implementation-summary Key Decision 'Default the unauthenticated MCP write surface to human… Automated callers must inject automated provenance context' show the human default is a documented, deliberate fail-protected decision on this single-user system, and the changelog's 'server-derived' claim remains true since derivation happens server-side.

p1.title: Trace observability can go stale on memory_search cache hits
p1.evidence: The cached path returns `buildSearchResponseFromPayload(cachedPayload, ...)` without recomputing trace-only observability. `vectorDegradation` is only built in the uncached pipeline path at lines 1249-1252, and causal-edge generation is only part of the cache key when causal boost is enabled at lines 952-954.
p1.why_real: `includeTrace` is supposed to report recall-time vector degradation and inline conflict state. A cached trace response can continue reporting the old vector health or old conflict warnings after the vector shard or causal edges change.
p1.suggested_fix: When `includeTrace` is true, either bypass cache for volatile trace fields or refresh `vectorDegradation` and conflict warnings after cache retrieval. Also include causal-edge generation and vector-health generation in the trace cache key.
p1.p2_reason: Refreshing volatile trace data on cache hits changes cache semantics and is not a minor local fix.

p1.title: Failed maintenance runs are not recorded as last-run errors
p1.evidence: The success path records `recordMaintenanceRun('memory_embedding_reconcile', ...)` at lines 79-89, but both error paths starting at line 97 return MCP errors without recording `lastStatus: 'error'`. The same pattern exists in retention sweep error handling.
p1.why_real: `memory_health` surfaces `getMaintenanceObservabilitySnapshot()`, so after a failed reconcile or sweep it can keep showing a previous success or null instead of the actual latest failed run.
p1.suggested_fix: Record `status: 'error'` in every early-abort and catch path for scan, reconcile, and retention handlers, with safe error/count metadata and the best available stale-candidate count.

p1.title: Folder boost can make why_ranked disagree with returned order
p1.evidence: Folder boost mutates `similarity` and re-sorts results by boosted similarity at lines 1117-1136. Later `buildWhyRankedTrace` selects `rrfScore` before `score` or `similarity` as the effective score, so the explanation can still report an unboosted fusion score for the final boosted order.
p1.why_real: A lower-fusion result can be boosted to rank 1 while `why_ranked.effectiveScore` still shows it below the next result. That violates the phase requirement that `why_ranked` cannot disagree with the actual returned ranking.
p1.suggested_fix: Carry the final post-boost sort score into the row before formatting, or include folder boost as an explicit ranker intermediate and have `why_ranked` prefer the final effective ranking score.
p1.v2_proof: memory-search.ts:1117-1138 mutates only `similarity` and re-sorts by it, while resolveEffectiveScore (retrieval-observability.ts:151-166) prefers intentAdjustedScore/rrfScore/stage2Score/score over similarity, so a boosted rank-1 row can report a lower why_ranked.effectiveScore than rank 2 — violating spec.md:117 REQ-001 ('the explanation cannot disagree with the ranking'); reachable on defaults since SPECKIT_FOLDER_DISCOVERY is graduated ON (ENV_REFERENCE.md:238) and memory-context.ts:1276-1279 sets factor 1.3.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Stamp the final post-boost sort score (or a folderBoost ranker intermediate) onto each row before buildWhyRankedTrace so effectiveScore reflects the order actually returned.

p1.title: Post-format dedup leaves rank traces and warnings inconsistent
p1.evidence: `formatSearchResults` stamps `why_ranked.rank` and inline warnings before return, then `handleMemorySearch` can filter results afterward via `deduplicateWithSessionState` at lines 1367-1370 and `applySessionDedup` at lines 1405-1422. Those branches update `data.results` and `data.count` but do not renumber `why_ranked` or prune/rebuild warning pairs.
p1.why_real: The final response can show the first returned result with `why_ranked.rank: 2`, or include an inline conflict warning referencing a document that was removed by dedup. That makes the observability payload untrue for the response actually delivered.
p1.suggested_fix: Run dedup before formatting, or after dedup renumber each remaining `why_ranked.rank` and rebuild/filter inline warnings against the final result set.
p1.v2_proof: search-results.ts:892 stamps why_ranked.rank from the pre-dedup array index and :1112-1127 embeds inlineWarnings computed over the full result set, then memory-search.ts:1367-1370 and :1405-1422 filter data.results/count post-format with no rank renumbering or warning pruning (zero why_ranked references in the handler); session dedup is on by default (enableDedup=true memory-search.ts:697; SESSION_CONFIG.enabled true unless DISABLE_SESSION_DEDUP, session-manager.ts:189).
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Run session dedup before formatSearchResults, or after dedup renumber surviving why_ranked.rank values and rebuild inlineWarnings against the final result set.

p1.title: Out-of-order vector rebuild completion can falsely clear degraded state
p1.evidence: `recordVectorShardRebuildCompleted` always calls `updateDegradedVectorHealth({ state: 'healthy', ... })` at lines 248-259. It only conditionally clears `activeJobId`, so a completion for an older or different job can set global state to healthy while another active rebuild remains.
p1.why_real: Concurrent or out-of-order rebuild events can make `memory_health` and `embedder_status` report a healthy vector state even though a different shard/job is still rebuilding or degraded.
p1.suggested_fix: Only transition to `healthy` when the completed job matches the active job or current degraded shard. Otherwise record the completion counter/event without clearing global degraded state, or track health per shard/job.
p1.v2_proof: retrieval-observability.ts:248-260 unconditionally sets state:'healthy' on any completion and only conditionally clears activeJobId, and concurrent repair jobs are reachable (enqueueJob runs each runJob async per jobId, reindex.ts:498-507; startup resume enqueues every queued/running repair job, reindex.ts:814-822) — a real race, but it requires two simultaneous rebuilds and only skews the in-memory memory_health/degraded-vector observability snapshot, not ranking or data.
p1.p2_reason: Out-of-order vector rebuild health semantics are state-machine sensitive and too risky for P2.

p1.title: Completed spec overclaims that no row IDs leak to consumers
p1.evidence: Formatted results still emit the memory row id at line 837, and the result interface includes id at line 98. This conflicts with spec.md line 118, which says all emitted identifiers resolve to path + anchor and no opaque row IDs leak to consumers.
p1.why_real: The shipped response still exposes opaque memory row IDs in the same retrieval surface. The implementation only keys the new why_ranked object to document path/anchor.
p1.suggested_fix: Either narrow the spec/changelog claim to the new observability diagnostics, or remove row IDs from trace/debug observability outputs if the requirement is intended literally.
p1.v2_proof: spec.md:60 scopes the rule to 'every surface here keys to a document path/anchor' and the Out-of-Scope table (spec.md §3) limits it to 'Row-ID-keyed traces'; the phase's new surfaces comply (WhyRankedTrace.document is path+anchor, retrieval-observability.ts:14-22,180; warning documents likewise at :358-361), while the result `id` at search-results.ts:837 is a pre-existing non-trace field outside this additive phase — REQ-002 read in context is not materially false.

p1.title: why_ranked ranks can disagree after session dedup filters formatted results
p1.evidence: formatSearchResults is called at line 1303, where search-results.ts:871-893 assigns why_ranked.rank from the pre-dedup array index. The post-cache session dedup block at lines 1376-1448 then filters data.results and updates count without renumbering or recomputing why_ranked.
p1.why_real: Session dedup is enabled by default for session-scoped calls. If the original rank-1 result is filtered, the first returned result can still report why_ranked.rank = 2, contradicting the phase claim that the trace explains the actual returned order.
p1.suggested_fix: Move session dedup before formatSearchResults or refresh why_ranked ranks after every post-format filter; add an includeTrace + session dedup regression where the top result is filtered.
p1.v2_proof: Same defect as idx 3: rank stamped pre-dedup at search-results.ts:892 (index+1), post-cache dedup at memory-search.ts:1405-1422 filters data.results without recomputing ranks (no why_ranked handling anywhere in the handler), and dedup defaults on for session-scoped calls (memory-search.ts:697, session-manager.ts:189), so the first returned result can carry why_ranked.rank:2 under includeTrace.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Move session dedup ahead of formatting or renumber why_ranked.rank after every post-format filter, with an includeTrace+dedup regression where the top result is filtered.

p1.title: Restore panel markdown bypasses the restored/omitted budget
p1.evidence: The budget loop fills `restored` and `omitted` at lines 362-377, but the returned `markdown` renders `renderContinuityFacets(facets)` directly instead of the budgeted `restored` items. `session-bootstrap.ts` then exposes that markdown in the startup payload at lines 358-364.
p1.why_real: A long handover or continuity field can be counted as omitted by the char budget while still being printed in the startup restore panel, making the fixed-budget and restored/not-restored claims materially false.
p1.suggested_fix: Render the panel from the budgeted `restored` list grouped by facet, include only omission summaries for omitted items, and add a test where an over-budget item is omitted and absent from `restorePanel.markdown`.

p1.title: Opt-in PreCompact snapshot wipes authored continuity fields (completion_pct, session_dedup, open/answered_questions)
p1.evidence: buildContinuityRecord (lines 109-128) hardcodes completion_pct: 0, open_questions: [], answered_questions: [] and never carries session_dedup. upsertThinContinuityInMarkdown -> mergeFrontmatterWithContinuity (thin-continuity-record.ts:913-929) replaces the whole _memory.continuity mapping with this record. The test fixture has completion_pct: 50 and the test (openltm-continuity-resilience.vitest.ts:216) only asserts last_updated_by changed — preservation is never checked.
p1.why_real: With SPECKIT_AUTHORED_CONTINUITY_SNAPSHOT=1, every PreCompact resets an authored completion_pct (e.g. 100 -> 0), deletes the session_dedup fingerprint that SPECKIT_COMPLETION_FRESHNESS validates against, and clears question state. This contradicts REQ-002 ('summarizes existing state'), REQ-003 ('com
p1.suggested_fix: In refreshAuthoredContinuitySnapshot, read the existing record via readThinContinuityRecord(currentSummary) and merge: preserve completion_pct, session_dedup, open_questions, answered_questions (and prefer existing blockers/key_files when ladder values are merely derived), updating only timestamp/ac

p1.title: PreCompact spec-folder detection turns spec doc file paths into nonexistent folders
p1.evidence: detectSpecFolder uses /\.opencode\/specs\/[\w/-]+/g, which excludes dots, then tries to strip a trailing file component only if the captured match still ends with /[^/]+\.\w+ at line 123.
p1.why_real: A common transcript path like .opencode/specs/.../009-openltm-continuity-resilience/implementation-summary.md is captured as .../implementation-summary, so the file-strip regex does not run and refreshAuthoredContinuitySnapshot receives a nonexistent spec folder. The opt-in snapshot can therefore sk
p1.suggested_fix: Include dots in the candidate match and strip known markdown file names, or resolve by walking upward to the nearest existing spec folder containing spec.md/description.json/graph-metadata.json.

p1.title: Restore panel renders unbudgeted facet content after computing omissions
p1.evidence: buildRestorePanel computes restored/omitted with max item and char budgets at lines 362-377, but the returned markdown renders renderContinuityFacets(facets), the original full facet set, at lines 393-398.
p1.why_real: Callers display restorePanel.markdown, so omitted over-budget facet text can still be shown even while omittedCount says it was not restored. This violates the fixed-budget restore panel invariant and can make restored/not-restored counts untrue.
p1.suggested_fix: Render markdown from the accepted restored items, or build a filtered ContinuityFacets object from restored before calling renderContinuityFacets.

p1.title: Authored snapshot refresh erases continuity completion and question state
p1.evidence: buildContinuityRecord creates a replacement ThinContinuityRecord with completion_pct: 0, open_questions: [], and answered_questions: [] at lines 124-126 before upserting it into implementation-summary.md.
p1.why_real: Refreshing a completed packet's authored continuity rewrites canonical _memory.continuity metadata from its previous completion/question state to zero/empty values. That changes the truthfulness of the packet-local continuity record rather than only refreshing a recovery snapshot.
p1.suggested_fix: Read the existing thin continuity record and preserve completion_pct, open_questions, answered_questions, and session_dedup unless the snapshot has explicit new values.

p1.title: Snapshot refresh exceptions abort the existing compact-cache path
p1.evidence: main calls refreshAuthoredContinuitySnapshot at lines 401-406 before entering the try block that builds and persists the compact cache at lines 415-441.
p1.why_real: If the opt-in snapshot path throws during synchronous file IO or ladder resolution, the top-level catch exits the hook without running the existing compact-cache work. An optional resilience feature can therefore cause loss of the normal PreCompact cache path.
p1.suggested_fix: Wrap refreshAuthoredContinuitySnapshot in its own try/catch, log the snapshot failure, and continue into the compact-cache merge/update path.
p1.v2_proof: compact-inject.ts:401-406 calls refreshAuthoredContinuitySnapshot outside the try at :415, the function does unguarded sync IO (authored-continuity-snapshot.ts:197,201,207,217), and main().catch at compact-inject.ts:491-494 exits before the cache build — structurally confirmed, but the feature is opt-in (authoredSnapshotEnabled, compact-inject.ts:133-137 requires SPECKIT_AUTHORED_CONTINUITY_SNAPSHOT=1 or an explicit input field) and the default path returns 'disabled' before any IO, so there is zero impact on a default configuration.
p1.p2_reason: Duplicate of snapshot refresh exception handling; fixed with isolated try/catch.

p1.title: Stop-hook CLI fallback reports deferred autosave without saving anything
p1.evidence: runContextAutosaveCliFallback invokes runWarmSpecMemoryCliTool with toolName: 'session_resume' and args { specFolder, sessionId, minimal: true } at lines 113-118, then returns 'deferred' on result.status === 'ok' at lines 119-121. The fallback is used when generate-context.js is missing or the primary autosave fails at lines 140-177.
p1.why_real: session_resume is a read/recovery call, not a continuity save. If the primary autosave path is missing or failed, this code can report a deferred autosave even though no save was performed or scheduled, making stop-hook state truthfully wrong and losing the intended session summary persistence.
p1.suggested_fix: Either call an actual save path that persists the payload, or keep the original failed/skipped outcome and log that CLI recovery was reachable but no autosave was performed.
p1.v2_proof: session-stop.ts:113-126 does call read-only session_resume (handlers/session-resume.ts:627-643 builds a minimal payload, no writes) and returns 'deferred', but the primary failure is still logged first (session-stop.ts:142,176), the outcome is consumed by no one (main() at :568 discards the result; 'deferred' appears nowhere else in hooks/tests), and the summary stays recoverable in hook state — so it's a misleading status label, not lost persistence or changed behavior.
p1.p2_reason: Changing stop-hook autosave fallback semantics is broader than a safe P2 patch.

p1.title: Spec-memory per-parameter numeric flags are not coerced
p1.evidence: parseValue only converts true/false/null and JSON-looking objects/arrays, otherwise returning the raw string at lines 298-311. The CLI help advertises per-flag usage such as memory_search --query ... --limit 3 at line 235, and validateCommand forwards parsed args into validateToolArgs at lines 506-516.
p1.why_real: Numeric CLI flags like --limit 3 arrive as the string "3" rather than a number. For numeric schemas this either rejects an advertised invocation path or forwards the wrong primitive type, so the per-parameter flag mode is not parity-equivalent to --json.
p1.suggested_fix: Add schema-driven coercion for number/integer/boolean values before validateToolArgs, matching the code-index CLI approach, or parse numeric primitives safely in parseValue.
p1.v2_proof: schemas/tool-input-schemas.ts:41-51 — validateToolArgs runs every arg through safeNumericPreprocess Zod schemas that coerce numeric strings client-side at spec-memory-cli.ts:516 before dispatch; live run `spec-memory memory_search --query "gold query battery" --limit 3` succeeded with exit 0 and results.

p1.title: Transport-down drill status is internally contradictory
p1.evidence: Line 38 says the phase completed with the T9xx multi-runtime transport-down drill passed, but line 104 says the final multi-runtime transport-down drill remains tracked as program-level verification. The parent spec also marks the T9xx drills passed.
p1.why_real: The shipped docs cannot simultaneously use the drill as completion evidence and list the same final drill as still open. This materially affects release-readiness truth.
p1.suggested_fix: Reconcile the actual drill artifact: if it passed, remove/update the open limitation; if it remains open, downgrade the completion/parent phase-map claims.
p1.v2_proof: Two different drills: the phase-level T9xx drill passed with concrete evidence (003-runtime-integration/tasks.md:72 — Claude hook 9/9, Codex hook 9/9, fail-open 1/1; plan.md:59), while implementation-summary.md:104 tracks the separate FINAL program-level drill whose ownership is explicitly documented at 004-release-and-program-cleanup/spec.md:83 ('owned by the workstream phases and the program gate'); frontmatter completion_pct 95 + next_safe_action 'Run final multi-runtime transport-down drill' show consistent deferral tracking, not contradiction.

p1.title: ENV verification lists a trust env var the CLI does not honor
p1.evidence: The summary lists MK_SKILL_ADVISOR_TRUST_DEFAULT among shipped CLI env vars, but skill-advisor-cli.ts only treats MK_SKILL_ADVISOR_CLI_TRUSTED or SPECKIT_SKILL_ADVISOR_CLI_TRUSTED as CLI trust defaults and rejects mutations without CLI-specific trust.
p1.why_real: An operator following the shipped CLI-env claim could set MK_SKILL_ADVISOR_TRUST_DEFAULT and still get CLI-side exit 64 for trusted mutations. The documentation overstates ENV parity.
p1.suggested_fix: Either teach the CLI to honor MK_SKILL_ADVISOR_TRUST_DEFAULT intentionally, or reclassify it as daemon-only and update the ENV count/verification text.
p1.v2_proof: ENV_REFERENCE.md:559 documents MK_SKILL_ADVISOR_TRUST_DEFAULT explicitly as '**Daemon-side** trust default... set in the committed MCP registrations; callers cannot forge it', and the daemon honors it (advisor-server.ts:211); the 004 summary's SC-002 claim is a documented-coverage tally ('0 missing, 0 phantom') that is true — no doc tells operators to set it for CLI-side trust, and skill-advisor-cli.ts:664 names the correct CLI vars in its refusal message.

p1.title: Default CLI sockets are service-global, so one worktree can talk to another worktree's daemon
p1.evidence: The CLI hardcodes DEFAULT_SOCKET_DIR = '/tmp/mk-spec-memory', sets SPECKIT_IPC_SOCKET_DIR to that value when absent at lines 753-756, then accepts any alive daemon at the resulting socket in ensureDaemonReady at lines 914-920. The code-index and skill-advisor CLIs repeat the same per-service global pattern with '/tmp/mk-code-index' and '/tmp/mk-skill-advisor'.
p1.why_real: With two checkouts/worktrees under the same user, the first live daemon on /tmp/mk-spec-memory/daemon-ipc.sock wins. A CLI invoked from the second checkout will probe the global socket, treat it as warm, and send tools/call to the first checkout's daemon/database, returning or mutating the wrong pro
p1.suggested_fix: Scope the default socket directory by repo/db identity, for example /tmp/mk-spec-memory-<repo-or-db-hash>, or make initialize/probe return daemon identity and reject mismatches before tools/call.
p1.v2_proof: Mechanics accurate — spec-memory-cli.ts:26,753-756 defaults SPECKIT_IPC_SOCKET_DIR to /tmp/mk-spec-memory, ensureDaemonReady (:914-922) accepts any alive daemon, and initialize (:937-944) checks only protocolVersion — but the identical global socket dirs are the committed repo convention pinned in all three runtime MCP registrations (opencode.json:27,58,79; .mcp.json:16,46,67; .codex/config.toml:68,96,114), so the CLI introduces no exposure beyond the deliberate shared-single-daemon architecture; missing daemon-identity verification is a hardening gap, not a new defect on default config.
p1.p2_reason: Socket identity scoping changes daemon connection semantics and need a dedicated design.

p1.title: Failed skill_graph_scan can render as completed in text mode
p1.evidence: runSkillGraphScanJob preserves the wrapped scan status and isError state at lines 1238-1258, but renderJobText always starts skill_graph_scan output with 'skill_graph_scan: completed' at line 910. renderPayload chooses this job text before any generic error/message rendering at lines 922-927.
p1.why_real: For --format text, a trusted scan that failed can still print a success-looking first line. The process exits nonzero, but human operators and log parsers often consume the text output, making the CLI's shipped status materially false.
p1.suggested_fix: Render the first line from payload.status/progress, e.g. 'failed' when any progress step is not ok or top-level status is error, and include the error before scan counters.
p1.v2_proof: skill-advisor-cli.ts:910 hardcodes the 'skill_graph_scan: completed' first line, renderPayload (:922-927) prefers jobText over the error branch, and runSkillGraphScanJob (:1243-1257) always supplies a data record (progress array) even when status='error'/isError=true — so a daemon-side scan failure that returns a payload prints a success-looking text line; however the exit code is still nonzero (:1295) and --format json carries the true status/error, limiting this to a text-rendering defect.
p1.p2_reason: skill_graph_scan text output now reports failed when payload/progress indicates failure.

p1.title: commandMap() rebuilt on every tool lookup and Levenshtein suggestion — O(n) per call with no caching
p1.evidence: function commandMap(): Map<string, ToolDefinition> { ... } called by getToolDefinition (line 274) and closestCommand (line 717). closestCommand iterates all 37 tools × 3 aliases = 111 Levenshtein comparisons per call, and each call to getToolDefinition (called per CLI invocation at line 392 and again at line 501 via validateCommand) rebuilds the 111-entry map from scratch.

In the hot path (parseCliArgs calls getToolDefinition, then validateCommand calls it again, then closestCommand rebuilds it yet again on unknown-command errors), this is 2-3 full map constructions per invocation. The s
p1.suggested_fix: Hoist commandMap() to a module-level lazy singleton: let _commandMap: Map<string, ToolDefinition> | null = null; function commandMap() { return _commandMap ??= buildCommandMap(); }
