The daemon's Zod schema may coerce it back, but spec-memory-cli.ts relies entirely on the daemon for type correction. If a daemon tool schema does not coerce strings to numbers, the filter i
p1.suggested_fix: Either add schema-aware coercion to spec-memory-cli.ts (matching code-index-cli.ts coerceArgsToSchema) or document the daemon-side coercion guarantee so the gap is intentional and verified.
p1.v2_proof: All three CLIs achieve numeric correctness, just at different layers: skill-advisor in parseValue (skill-advisor-cli.ts:281-284), code-index in coerceArgsToSchema (code-index-cli.ts:~532 with an explicit rationale comment), and spec-memory via safeNumericPreprocess Zod coercion inside the shared validateToolArgs it calls client-side (tool-input-schemas.ts:41-51, spec-memory-cli.ts:516) — the 'if the daemon does not coerce' premise is false (same schemas daemon-side), and the live `--limit 3` invocation succeeded.

p1.title: Deep-review auto setup writes config to a fixed review path instead of the resolved artifact_dir
p1.evidence: Auto setup persists to `{spec_folder}/review/deep-review-config.json`, but the same presentation later says child phases/sub-phases output to `{spec_folder}/review/{packet}-pt-{NN}/` and contracts outputs under `{artifact_dir}` at lines 247 and 252.
p1.why_real: Repeated or nested review runs can place the config in a different directory than the rest of the packet, or overwrite the flat review config while the artifacts are supposed to live under a pt-NN artifact directory.
p1.suggested_fix: Resolve `artifact_dir` before config persistence and write `deep-review-config.json` under `{artifact_dir}` everywhere, including the auto setup path.
p1.p2_reason: Real artifact_dir inconsistency; deep-review auto setup now documents config persistence under resolved artifact_dir.

p1.title: Attached :auto/:confirm syntax is documented as canonical but has no command file to resolve
p1.evidence: The router says to parse attached command suffixes and declares `/deep:start-review-loop:auto` / `/deep:start-review-loop:confirm` canonical, while only `.opencode/commands/deep/start-review-loop.md` exists. The context-loop file repeats the same pattern at `.opencode/commands/deep/start-context-loop.md:78`, with only `start-context-loop.md` present.
p1.why_real: If OpenCode resolves slash commands by command file path, the attached examples route to non-existent command names before this router can parse `$ARGUMENTS`, so documented auto/confirm invocations fail or never reach the mode router.
p1.suggested_fix: Use argument suffix syntax consistently, e.g. `/deep:start-review-loop "target" :auto`, or add real command aliases/files for the attached forms and keep examples, argument hints, and router parsing synchronized.
p1.v2_proof: start-review-loop.md:77 (and start-research-loop.md:86, start-context-loop.md, ask-ai-council.md) deliberately declares the attached form the canonical synchronized entrypoint, the failure mode rests on the seat's own unverified conditional ('If OpenCode resolves slash commands by command file path'), the argument-hint (line 3) documents the working space-separated [:auto|:confirm] token form that reaches $ARGUMENTS regardless, and a missing suffix degrades to execution_mode=ASK (line 81), not failure.

p1.title: Deep-context offers a standalone run directory even though the router requires spec_folder before YAML load
p1.evidence: Q1 offers `E) None yet — use a standalone run dir`, but `.opencode/commands/deep/start-context-loop.md:62-64` requires `spec_folder` to be bound before YAML load, and this presentation's save/output contract still describes canonical spec-doc continuity under a spec folder at lines 226-236.

p1.suggested_fix: Either remove option E from this command or introduce a distinct `artifact_dir`/`standalone` mode through the router, YAML start condition, output contract, and save phase.
p1.v2_proof: deep_start-context-loop_auto.yaml:51 explicitly types the required binding as 'Spec folder path (or standalone run dir)', line 161 fail-closes standalone only when a real spec folder is derivable, and line 697 skips the canonical continuity save when spec_folder is a standalone run dir ('hand the report path to /speckit:plan instead') — both claimed failure modes (start-condition failure, continuity refresh against a non-spec dir) are explicitly handled (confirm YAML lines 46/152/743 mirror this).

p1.title: Parent review completion is over-claimed

The committed parent review report still scopes itself to 4 families at review/review-report.md:3 and remains CONDITIONAL with active P1s at review/review-report.md:10 and review/review-report.md:38. p1.title: Parent continuity omits the shipped deep child
p1.evidence: The continuity block says "All 4 command families shipped" and key_files lists only 001-004 at spec.md:20-23, while the same spec's phase map includes 005-deep-commands as Complete at spec.md:122 and graph-metadata includes the 005 child.

p1.title: Optional latency gate checks mean while the success criterion is p95
p1.evidence: The test records both `cte_p95_ms` and `bfs_p95_ms`, but when `SPECKIT_BENCH_GATE=1` it asserts `bfs.mean <= cte.mean` instead of comparing p95. The phase spec's SC-002 is explicitly p95-at-or-below-current-CTE.
p1.why_real: With the gate enabled, a run where BFS p95 regresses but mean improves would pass, so the optional enforcement does not validate the documented success criterion.
p1.suggested_fix: Gate on `bfs.p95 <= cte.p95` for SC-002, and keep the mean comparison as additional advisory output if desired.
p1.v2_proof: causal-traversal-bfs-equivalence.vitest.ts:264-268 does assert bfs.mean<=cte.mean under SPECKIT_BENCH_GATE=1 while spec.md:119 SC-002 specifies p95 — mechanically true, but the gate is opt-in (never default/CI) and implementation-summary.md:84/124 explicitly documents the comparison as advisory-not-gated, so this is a test-alignment nit, not a runtime or verification-integrity defect.

p1.title: Typed causal boost mis-scores incoming undirected edges
p1.evidence: The BFS reader treats causal edges as undirected, including incoming rows as neighbor edges, but the typed traversal relation lookup only searches `source_id IN (seedIds) AND target_id = neighborId`. For an edge `neighbor -> seed`, the neighbor is discovered but this lookup misses the actual relation and falls back to `caused`.
p1.why_real: When typed traversal is enabled, relation priority changes the active boost. Incoming `supersedes` or `contradicts` edges therefore get neutral scoring instead of their real relation, changing ranking/injection behavior for valid one-hop causal neighbors.
p1.suggested_fix: Lookup the first-hop relation in both directions, e.g. `(source_id IN (...) AND target_id = ?) OR (target_id IN (...) AND source_id = ?)`, with matching bind parameters. If 2-hop relation scoring is intended, carry relation evidence out of the traversal instead of inferring it later.
p1.v2_proof: bfs-traversal.ts:232-278 reads causal edges as an explicitly undirected adjacency (incoming rows reversed at lines 268-277) so neighbors are discovered via neighbor→seed edges, but causal-boost.ts:533-538 looks up the relation only as 'source_id IN (seedIds) AND target_id = neighborId', so incoming-edge neighbors miss and fall back to 'caused' (line 530), giving wrong intent edge-priors for incoming supersedes/contradicts edges under default-on SPECKIT_TYPED_TRAVERSAL + SPECKIT_CAUSAL_BOOST (flags default enabled per rollout-policy isFeatureEnabled, undefined→true).
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Query the first-hop relation in both directions ((source IN seeds AND target=?) OR (target IN seeds AND source=?)) or carry relation evidence out of the BFS walk.

p1.title: Memo cycle checks can be bypassed by multiple store instances
p1.evidence: `dependencyEdgeCount` is counted once at construction and the code only calls `wouldCreateCycle` when that cached count is greater than zero. A store constructed while the table is empty keeps `dependencyEdgeCount = 0`; if another store inserts `A -> B`, this stale store can insert `B -> A` without any reachability check.
p1.why_real: The exported factory can create multiple `MemoStore` instances over the same database, and the database constraint only blocks self-edges. This regresses the cycle invariant from the old query-on-insert behavior and can persist multi-node cycles.
p1.suggested_fix: Do not let a stale zero cache suppress correctness checks. Refresh the edge count before using the zero-row fast path, make the store singleton-owned, or move cycle prevention into a transaction/DB-backed check that sees current `dependency_edges`.
p1.v2_proof: Duplicate of idx 3/5: the single-writer invariant is documented at memo.ts:103-104 and no production path creates two concurrent MemoStore instances or calls addDependencyEdge at all (incremental-index.ts:741 is the only non-test instantiation, short-lived and synchronous), so the cycle-bypass requires a usage pattern absent from shipped code.

p1.title: Injected neighbor fetch is unbounded and unhandled
p1.evidence: `fetchNeighborRows` builds one `IN (...)` placeholder per `injectId`, and `applyCausalBoost` passes every non-existing active boost into it without chunking or a try/catch.
p1.why_real: A dense two-hop traversal can produce thousands of injectable neighbors. Once the placeholder count exceeds SQLite's bind limit, the SELECT throws outside the traversal fail-open path and can fail the whole search request.
p1.suggested_fix: Chunk `fetchNeighborRows` with the same safe binding size used by the BFS reader, or cap injectable IDs before SQL and fail open on fetch errors.
p1.v2_proof: applyCausalBoost's only production caller wraps it in try/catch at stage2-fusion.ts:1076-1092 (warn + metadata.causalBoostApplied='failed', results preserved), so a fetchNeighborRows throw degrades gracefully and cannot 'fail the whole search request'; injectable IDs are also bounded by the 2-hop walk from ≤5 seeds, far below SQLite's bind limit in practice.

p1.title: SC-002 p95 latency PASS is not actually gated by the shipped test
p1.evidence: spec.md:119 requires hot-path traversal p95 at or below the current CTE, and the changelog marks `Latency regression | PASS after de-flake` at changelog-012-causal-traversal-bfs.md:44. The test logs p95 values at causal-traversal-bfs-equivalence.vitest.ts:263, but the only assertion under SPECKIT_BENCH_GATE checks `bfs.mean <= cte.mean` at lines 264-265; default mode only warns on mean at lines 266-268. implementation-summary.md:130 also says p95 values overlapped and each side was above the oth
p1.why_real: The shipped docs present the p95 latency criterion/regression as passed, but the committed test never fails on p95 regression and the recorded evidence admits p95 is not consistently at or below CTE. This is a material docs-vs-verification mismatch.
p1.suggested_fix: Either amend SC-002/changelog to state latency is advisory and mean-gated only under SPECKIT_BENCH_GATE, or change the benchmark gate to assert the documented p95 criterion and update the implementation summary with fresh passing evidence.
p1.v2_proof: True that the gate asserts mean only (causal-traversal-bfs-equivalence.vitest.ts:264-265) and spec.md:119 keeps the p95 criterion under Status: Shipped, but implementation-summary.md:124/130 transparently discloses that the comparison is advisory and p95 overlapped run-to-run, and the changelog's 'Latency regression | PASS after de-flake' describes the test outcome — a stale SC-002 wording to amend, not a deceptive verification claim.
p1.p2_reason: The shipped test now gates the documented p95 criterion; spec docs were not edited.

p1.title: MemoStore zero-edge cache can miss edges written by another store
p1.evidence: dependencyEdgeCount is counted once at construction and maintained only on this instance's inserts (lines 103-115, 125-132). addDependencyEdge skips wouldCreateCycle when dependencyEdgeCount is 0 (line 210), and collectDependents returns [] on the same stale zero (lines 217-223).
p1.why_real: MemoStore/createMemoStore are exported and nothing enforces the single-writer assumption. If another MemoStore or direct DB writer inserts dependency_edges after this instance was constructed with zero edges, this instance misses dependent invalidation and can insert a cycle that a current-table tra
p1.suggested_fix: Do not use the cached zero as a correctness gate unless single-writer is enforced. Refresh the count before zero fast-path decisions when count is 0, or centralize dependency edge writes through one store/singleton and add a multi-store regression test.
p1.v2_proof: Duplicate of idx 3/5/10: memo.ts:103-104 documents the single-writer assumption, addDependencyEdge/collectDependents have no production callers outside the per-call store created at incremental-index.ts:741 (planMemoizedIndexing, itself not yet wired into the scan handler), and better-sqlite3's synchronous single-process execution means the cached count cannot go stale within the only lifetime the store ever has today.

p1.title: Changelog mislabels restart durability as deferred REQ-003
p1.evidence: The changelog says "Durability across a restart mid-repair is the deferred REQ-003 work, shipped in phase 020", but the phase spec defines REQ-003 as the KNN scalar JOIN vs vec0 MATCH live-corpus benchmark at spec.md:111.
p1.why_real: This is a materially false traceability claim: it can make readers believe the live-corpus KNN benchmark deferral was shipped in phase 020, when the referenced requirement is not restart durability.
p1.suggested_fix: Reword the follow-up to say restart durability was separate follow-on work shipped in phase 020, and keep REQ-003 tied only to the live-corpus KNN benchmark deferral.

p1.title: Repair completion renames the staged shard over the still-attached live shard, leaving the daemon serving (and writing to) an unlinked stale inode
p1.evidence: runJob unlinks the live shard's -wal/-shm and `fs.renameSync(stagingShardPath, activeShardPath)` (reindex.ts:613-624) while the main connection still has that shard ATTACHED as active_vec. The follow-up `attachActiveVectorShard(jobDb, targetProfile)` (reindex.ts:667-669) takes the `attachedPath === path.resolve(shardPath)` branch in vector-index-store.ts:1501 (PRAGMA database_list still reports the original attach path), so it never detaches/re-attaches — the connection keeps the old, now-unlink
p1.why_real: After an in-process auto-repair, all subsequent vector writes go through the temp-view aliases into active_vec — the orphaned old inode — and silently vanish on restart (the on-disk file is the staged shard, which lacks them). The live attachment's vec_memories KNN table is also empty post-repair (w
p1.suggested_fix: In the repair-completion path, detach active_vec (after a WAL checkpoint) before the staging rename, then re-attach — or make attachActiveVectorShard force a detach/re-attach when the on-disk inode differs from the attached one (e.g. compare fs.statSync(shardPath).ino against a recorded attach-time
p1.v2_proof: reindex.ts:626-634 now calls detachActiveVectorShard(jobDb) BEFORE fs.renameSync (line 645), with a comment naming the exact inode hazard; added in remediation commit 8bf4c89813.

p1.title: Rebuilt shards are missing a table that the read-path integrity probe requires
p1.evidence: `ensureShardSchema()` creates only `vec_metadata` and `vec_<dim>` for a staged shard, while `required_vector_shard_tables()` in `vector-index-store.ts:483-489` requires `embedding_cache` for every shard probe. A shard produced by the repair/reindex path can therefore be treated as malformed on the next attach.
p1.why_real: This breaks the claimed quarantine -> rebuild -> healthy flow: the reindex output does not satisfy the store's own integrity contract, so a successfully rebuilt shard can be quarantined again or replaced with a fresh empty shard.
p1.suggested_fix: Create `embedding_cache` in the reindex staging shard using the same schema as `ensure_embedding_cache_table()`, or remove it from the required probe set if it is not actually required for read correctness.
p1.v2_proof: reindex.ts:423-433 ensureShardSchema creates only vec_metadata + vec_<dim> (plus vec_memories at 477-481) while required_vector_shard_tables (vector-index-store.ts:483-490) demands embedding_cache, so the post-swap attach probe (vector-index-store.ts:1548-1551) quarantines the just-rebuilt shard — reproduced live: `npx vitest run tests/embedders/reindex-durability-cancel.vitest.ts` fails DR-020 happy path on HEAD (expected 3 shard rows, got 2, with the auto-scheduled repair loop churning).
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Create embedding_cache in ensureShardSchema using the ensure_embedding_cache_table shape (or drop it from required_vector_shard_tables), then re-green the DR-020 happy-path test.

p1.title: Same-profile repair swaps the shard file while the active SQLite attachment still points at the old file
p1.evidence: `runJob()` renames the staging shard over `activeShardPath` at lines 613-624, then calls `attachActiveVectorShard()` at lines 667-669. In the same-profile repair case, `active_vec` is already attached to that path; `attachActiveVectorShard()` sees `attachedPath === shardPath` at `vector-index-store.ts:1501` and probes/reuses the existing attachment instead of detaching and reopening the newly renamed file.
p1.why_real: After a repair of the currently active shard, the process can mark the rebuild complete while reads continue using the old attached database handle, typically the fresh empty shard created after quarantine. That makes health say healthy while vector search can still miss rebuilt vectors until a clea
p1.suggested_fix: Detach `active_vec` before replacing the active shard, or force `attachActiveVectorShard()` to detach and reattach after any completed shard swap before recording repair completion.
p1.v2_proof: reindex.ts:626-634 detaches active_vec before the rename, so the post-swap attachActiveVectorShard call (reindex.ts:689-691) takes the fresh-attach branch, not the attachedPath===shardPath reuse branch at vector-index-store.ts:1501; fixed in 8bf4c89813.

p1.title: Active-embedder searches bypass vectors written by normal indexing
p1.evidence: When an active embedder is configured, getActiveVectorSourceForQuery selects active_vec.vec_${active.dim} if that table exists (lines 103-110), and attachActiveVectorShard creates that vec_<dim> table on every shard attach (vector-index-store.ts:802-808). Normal index/update paths only write active_vec.vec_memories (vector-index-mutations.ts:325-330 and 599-603), while vector_search joins the selected source table (vector-index-queries.ts:444-449). Startup configures/attaches the active embedder
p1.why_real: In the normal runtime with an active embedder, newly saved successful embeddings are inserted into vec_memories but vector_search reads vec_<dim>, so those records become invisible to vector similarity search until a reindex happens to populate vec_<dim>.
p1.suggested_fix: Either keep active vector reads on active_vec.vec_memories, or make index/update/delete dual-write vec_memories and vec_<dim> atomically. Add an active-embedder regression test where index_memory followed by vector_search returns the inserted row.
p1.v2_proof: vector-index-queries.ts:103-121 selects active_vec.vec_<dim> as the query source (joined at 444-449) when a non-default embedder is active, but every runtime write path inserts only vec_memories — vector-index-mutations.ts:331-337 and 604-609, retry-manager.ts:762, reconsolidation.ts:345, lineage-state.ts:558 — and ensure_vector_shard_schema (vector-index-store.ts:802-808) guarantees vec_<dim> exists on every attach, so post-swap saves are invisible to vector search until the next reindex.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Dual-write vec_memories and vec_<dim> in index/update/delete (or pin active-embedder reads to vec_memories), plus a regression test asserting index_memory→vector_search round-trip under an active embedder.

p1.title: Integrity health checks can pass while the active vector-search table is empty
p1.evidence: verify_integrity computes missing_vectors and total_vectors only from activeVectorSchema('vec_memories') (lines 1557-1568), but active-embedder vector_search can read active_vec.vec_<dim> instead (lines 103-110 and 444-449). memory_health then reports vecRowsTotal from verifyIntegrity (memory-crud-health.ts:986-990).
p1.why_real: The new degraded-vector health surface can report no missing vectors because vec_memories is populated even when the actual table used by vector_search has no rows, hiding the read-path regression instead of surfacing it.
p1.suggested_fix: Make verify_integrity inspect the same vector source selected for queries, or report and compare both vec_memories and vec_<dim> counts. Add a health test with active embedder state where the two tables diverge.
p1.v2_proof: vector-index-queries.ts:1557-1567 computes missing_vectors/total_vectors solely from active_vec.vec_memories while the query source can be vec_<dim> (103-121); real mechanism but observability-only, manifests only under a non-default active embedder, and is a corollary of idx 3.
p1.p2_reason: Vector integrity source selection affects health semantics and needs broader test coverage.

p1.title: Changelog claims byte-identical ranking parity, but tests prove packed intentionally differs from legacy

The committed test asserts `legacyTop` is not `101`, `packedTop` is `101`, and flat packed weighting is not `101`; the comparison helper only checks packed metrics are at least legacy and `titleSignalRestored`, not byte equality.

p1.title: Comparison helper ignores the configured NDCG cutoff
p1.evidence: BM25BaselineConfig documents `k` as the top-K cutoff for NDCG and Recall at line 43, and runBM25EngineComparison accepts `config.k` at lines 219-224, but runComparisonMetrics always calls `computeNDCG(evalResults, groundTruth, 10)`.
p1.why_real: Any consumer passing a non-default `k` gets Recall@k but still gets NDCG@10 under the backwards-compatible `ndcg10` field, which can skew regression comparisons and phase evidence.
p1.suggested_fix: Either pass `k` into `computeNDCG` for the comparison helper or split the API so NDCG remains explicitly fixed at 10 while only Recall uses the configurable cutoff.

p1.title: Checkpoint restore skips cache/index invalidation when clearExisting removes data but restores zero rows
p1.evidence: handleCheckpointRestore passes clear_existing into restoreCheckpoint at line 549, but cache clearing, BM25 rebuild, and trigger cache refresh only run when result.restored > 0 or result.workingMemoryRestored > 0 at line 560. The success hints still report 'Previous data was cleared before restore' and 'Search indexes rebuilt' at lines 649-651.
p1.why_real: A clearExisting restore can change the live dataset by deleting existing memory even when the checkpoint contains zero restored rows. In that case stale vector caches, trigger cache, and the existing BM25 singleton can continue serving pre-restore results until another rebuild or restart.
p1.suggested_fix: Run cache invalidation and index reset/rebuild whenever clear_existing is true or any restore count is nonzero. For zero-row restores, clear/reset indexes instead of reporting that they were rebuilt.
p1.v2_proof: handlers/checkpoints.ts:560 gates cache/BM25/trigger invalidation on restored>0||workingMemoryRestored>0 while the clearExisting delete commits regardless (lib/storage/checkpoints.ts:3016-3043, full-table fallback at 1643-1652) and hints at 649-651 still claim 'Search indexes rebuilt'; mechanism real but requires the narrow zero-row-checkpoint + clearExisting=true combination.

p1.title: FakeVectorStore accepts vector searches the real port rejects or filters differently
p1.evidence: FakeVectorStore.search defaults the filter to `options.minScore ?? Number.NEGATIVE_INFINITY` and `cosineSimilarity` only compares `Math.min(left.length, right.length)`, while BetterSqliteVectorStore validates exact embedding dimension and defaults the port overload's missing `minScore` to 0. The contract suite always passes `minScore: 0`, so it never exercises the default or a dimension mismatch.

p1.title: FakeContentionPolicy skips configured backoff hooks
p1.evidence: FakeContentionPolicy computes `delayMs` and calls `onRetry`, then immediately checks `shouldAbort` without invoking `options.sleep` or `options.sleepSync`. The real BetterSqliteContentionPolicy awaits/synchronously runs the configured sleep hooks, and file-watcher retry consumers pass a custom abortable sleep.
p1.why_real: A fake-based integration test cannot validate abortable retry sleeps or sync backoff behavior, so regressions in retry shutdown/backoff semantics can be hidden while the port contract still passes.
p1.suggested_fix: Honor `sleep`/`sleepSync` in FakeContentionPolicy, including the `sync` path, and add a contract test that proves the sleep hook runs before the next attempt and before the post-delay abort check.
p1.v2_proof: Confirmed in code — fakes/storage-ports.ts:287-339 computes delayMs and calls onRetry but never invokes options.sleep/sleepSync, while the real policy awaits (options.sleep ?? this.sleep)(delayMs) at contention-policy.ts:135 and the sync hook at line 163 — a genuine fake/real parity gap, but test-infrastructure-only (abort ordering is otherwise preserved), so no current runtime correctness impact.

p1.title: code-index and skill-advisor stale guards are not initialized by their documented builds

The shims can then keep exiting 69 after the documented rebuild command, breaking the C

p1.suggested_fix: Update production mutation consumers to call addDocumentFields(String(id), buildBm25DocumentFields(row)) or add a field-aware public helper, then add an integration test that a saved/updated title hit ranks the same as the same row indexed through rebuildFromDatabase/syncChangedRows.

p1.title: RSS budget gate tests sync warmup path, not production async batched path
p1.evidence: Test iterates `iterateCorpusSizedDocuments()` synchronously calling `addDocumentFields` in a tight loop (line 122-128). Production warmup uses `rebuildFromDatabase` (bm25-index.ts:760) which batches via `syncChangedRows` with async `setTimeout(..., 0)` between batches. The implementation-summary.md claims 'peak-sampled RSS spike 136.5 MB ≤ 150 MB' (line 91) and changelog says 'always-on gate' (line 35), but the gate only asserts the sync path.
p1.why_real: The async batched path yields to the event loop between batches, giving V8 opportunities to run GC and decommit transient pages. The sync path holds all transient allocations without yielding. The 136.5 MB measurement may understate or overstate the production spike; the hard gate cannot catch a reg
p1.suggested_fix: Add a test that uses `rebuildFromDatabase` (with `vi.useFakeTimers` + `vi.runAllTimersAsync`) and measures peak RSS across batch boundaries, or document that the sync-path RSS is a conservative upper bound with evidence.

The same helper is used from ensureSchemaMigrations (lines 303-306).

p1.title: Dead-socket respawn can signal a reused, unrelated PID
p1.evidence: resolveRespawnTargetPid trusts persisted childPid/ownerPid from the lease file (lines 584-587), then reapOwnerBeforeRespawn sends SIGTERM/SIGKILL to that PID (lines 404-418) with no executable, start-time, or socket-owner validation.
p1.why_real: If a stale lease remains after the advisor process exits and the OS reuses that PID, the respawn path can terminate an unrelated same-user process. This is a real concurrent stale-state hazard and can cause data loss outside the advisor daemon.
p1.suggested_fix: Persist child process identity evidence in the lease, such as executable path plus start time/ctime where available, and verify it before sending signals; otherwise refuse respawn and report the stale lease.
p1.v2_proof: .opencode/bin/mk-skill-advisor-launcher.cjs:584-588 trusts lease childPid/ownerPid and :404-418 SIGTERM/SIGKILLs with only kill(pid,0) liveness checks (no executable/start-time identity validation), but firing requires a crash-stale lease + OS PID reuse to a same-user process + a confirmed-dead socket behind N-retry deep probes and the bootstrap-lock re-read at :618-626 — a hardening gap, not default-config behavior today.
p1.p2_reason: PID identity hardening is non-trivial launcher safety work and not P2-safe.

p1.title: Absolute advisor DB override is joined under workspaceRoot and ignored
p1.evidence: SKILL_GRAPH_DB becomes join(advisorDbDirOverride, 'skill-graph.sqlite') when MK_SKILL_ADVISOR_DB_DIR or SYSTEM_SKILL_ADVISOR_DB_DIR is set (lines 41-44), but loadSqliteProjection then computes join(workspaceRoot, SKILL_GRAPH_DB) (line 272). In Node, path.join('/repo', '/tmp/db/skill-graph.sqlite') normalizes to '/repo/tmp/db/skill-graph.sqlite', not the override path.
p1.why_real: Any absolute sandbox or operator DB override makes the recommend path look in the wrong location, fall back to filesystem projection, and silently drop SQLite-only data such as indexed doc triggers and graph state.
p1.suggested_fix: Use the override path as-is when SKILL_GRAPH_DB is absolute, for example path.isAbsolute(SKILL_GRAPH_DB) ? SKILL_GRAPH_DB : join(workspaceRoot, SKILL_GRAPH_DB).
p1.v2_proof: projection.ts:42-44 + :272 (join(workspaceRoot, SKILL_GRAPH_DB) mangles absolute overrides; write path skill-graph-db.ts:248-252 resolves them correctly), but the committed default override is RELATIVE (opencode.json:48 ".opencode/skills/.../database") which joins correctly, and advisor-status.ts:31-33/:198 uses the same path so an absolute override yields a loud 'absent' refusal, not silent data drop — manifests only on non-default absolute overrides.
p1.p2_reason: Duplicate absolute DB override issue fixed in projection path resolution.

p1.title: advisor_recommend persists raw user prompts in the default shadow log
p1.evidence: Lines 378-387 call recordShadowDelta with `prompt: input.prompt` for each shadow recommendation. The sink appends the whole record as JSONL to the default `.opencode/skills/system-skill-advisor/mcp_server/data/shadow-deltas.jsonl`, so prompt text containing secrets is stored persistently even though response attribution is sanitized.
p1.why_real: This is a recommend-path side effect and a privacy/security regression: users commonly paste credentials, tokens, private paths, or proprietary text into prompts, and the shipped prompt-safe response contract does not prevent those raw prompts from being written to disk.
p1.suggested_fix: Do not store raw prompts by default. Store a keyed hash or redacted/canonical feature summary instead, and gate raw prompt logging behind an explicit debug-only opt-in with clear retention handling.
p1.v2_proof: advisor-recommend.ts:379-387 passes prompt: input.prompt unconditionally and shadow-sink.ts:97-100 appends it to the default JSONL (file exists, gitignored at .gitignore:81), but the shipped redaction contract covers RESPONSES (README.md:35,59) which is upheld — local gitignored prompt telemetry in a local-first tool whose memory system already persists session content is a P2 retention/redaction improvement, not a P1 security regression.

p1.title: Adopting a responsive stale child leaves the secondary proxy holding the owner lease
p1.evidence: checkStrictSingleWriter acquires the owner lease and starts the heartbeat before stale launcher-lease handling at lines 702-709. In the stale live child branch, a successful bridge returns true at lines 733-735 without clearing that owner lease; only the non-bridge/non-respawn branch clears it at lines 737-740.

Later launchers take the owner-lease path and leaseResultForOwnerLease can synthesize a no-socket lease for that proxy, so additional sessions can be blocked from bridging to t
p1.suggested_fix: When the stale child is adopted via bridge, release the temporary owner lease and heartbeat before returning, or transfer ownership to a lease record that accurately points at a bridgeable daemon/socket. Keep the current owner lease only for the respawn path that actually launches the replacement ch
p1.v2_proof: mk-skill-advisor-launcher.cjs:732-741 does keep the owner lease after bridge-adoption, but the claimed blocking does not occur: launcher-ipc-bridge.cjs:393-399 falls back to getIpcSocketPath(serviceName,{dbDir}) when leaseResult.socketPath is null/unusable, so later sessions probe the daemon's well-known socket and bridge normally; holding the lease is the intended adoption semantics (prevents a competing launcher from stale-reclaiming and double-spawning over the live adopted daemon), and process.on('exit', clearAllLeaseFiles) at :1185 releases it when the proxy session ends.

p1.title: Reap helper can allow replacement spawn without confirming the old child exited
p1.evidence: `reapOwnerBeforeRespawn` sends SIGKILL and awaits `waitForPidExit(ownerPid, 1000)`, but ignores the boolean result and returns `{ allowed: true }` at line 420. Both respawn paths rely on that allowed result before replacement launch (`respawnAfterDeadSocket` around line 629 and stale bootstrap cleanup around line 1202), while the spec/summary claim the old child is reaped before replacement spawn.
p1.why_real: If a stale or wedged child remains live past the grace window, the launcher can proceed to spawn a replacement while the old process is still alive, weakening the single-writer/resource-safety guarantee this phase claims to close.
p1.suggested_fix: After the SIGKILL wait, recheck liveness and return `allowed:false` or report without spawning when the old pid is still alive; add a test that exercises the non-exiting reap path.
p1.v2_proof: mk-skill-advisor-launcher.cjs:418-420 ignores the post-SIGKILL waitForPidExit result and returns {allowed:true}, but a SIGKILL'd pid can only linger as a zombie (which kill(pid,0) reports alive yet cannot write) or transient D-state, so the single-writer exposure is marginal and returning allowed:false on a zombie would wedge respawn permanently — hardening nit, not a P1.
p1.p2_reason: Reap helper now refuses replacement spawn if SIGKILL wait still cannot confirm exit.

p1.title: Dead-socket respawn can abort after killing the old daemon when model-server cleanup is slow
p1.evidence: respawnAfterDeadSocket waits only 1000ms for previousOwner.ownerPid before trying writeOwnerLeaseFileExclusive at lines 636-653. The old launcher's child-exit path awaits shutdownModelServerForLauncherExit before clearing leases at lines 1114-1124, and shutdownModelServerRoot can wait the 7000ms grace path at lines 921-945.

p1.title: Interrupted reindex can publish a shard containing only the tail rows
p1.evidence: runJob resumes from the persisted processed count (line 535), deletes any staging shard at start (line 567), selects the next batch with OFFSET processed (line 579), writes only those remaining rows to the staging shard (line 591), then renames that staging file over the active shard (line 623).
p1.why_real: After a crash or restore pause with processed > 0, the resumed job starts from an empty staging file but skips the already-processed prefix. Completion then publishes a shard missing those vectors, while the job can still be marked completed and repair sentinels cleared, causing durable recall loss.
p1.suggested_fix: When staging is recreated, rebuild from offset 0 and reset progress, or preserve and validate the existing staging shard before resuming from processed. Do not swap a staging shard unless its rowcount covers the intended completed dataset.
p1.v2_proof: reindex.ts:548 resumes processed from the persisted row, :580 cleanupStaging() deletes any prior staging, :592 selects batches with OFFSET processed, and :626-645 renames the staging shard over the active shard guarded only by fs.existsSync with no row-coverage check — so a job resumed via resumeReindexJobs (801-825) or the restore-pause requeue (693-701) with processed>0 swaps in a shard missing rows [0,processed) and completeVectorShardRecovery (677-682) still clears the repair sentinel.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Reset processed to 0 whenever staging is recreated (or preserve/validate existing staging), and refuse the swap unless staged rowcount covers the full intended dataset.

p1.title: Repair de-duplication does not survive process restart
p1.evidence: repair context is stored only in the in-memory vectorShardRepairJobs map (lines 123 and 742-744). resumeReindexJobs reloads queued/running jobs from embedder_jobs but does not rebuild that map (lines 777-792), and startVectorShardRepairReindex de-dupes only by scanning the map (lines 809-821).
p1.why_real: After a crash with a queued/running repair job persisted, a restart can resume the old job and also schedule a new repair from the sentinel because the same-shard guard has forgotten the old repair context. That violates the claimed one same-shard in-flight repair invariant and can race duplicate sh
p1.suggested_fix: Persist repair metadata with the job row or a side table, reload it during resume, and de-dupe against persisted queued/running repair jobs before creating a new repair job.
p1.v2_proof: reindex.ts:176-186 adds persisted repair_reason/repair_shard_path columns, :764-768 writes them at job creation, resumeReindexJobs rebuilds the in-memory map from them (:813-820), and startVectorShardRepairReindex de-dupes against persisted queued/running rows first (:842-854); fixed in 8bf4c89813.

p1.title: Marker-less incomplete shards still attach as healthy
p1.evidence: assess_vector_shard_repair_state computes shardComplete from success-count versus vector rows (lines 635-642), but handle_vector_shard_repair_assessment only acts when a sentinel exists or orphan quarantine artifacts exist (lines 677-691). attachActiveVectorShard then attaches/creates the shard and ensures schema anyway (lines 1562-1585).
p1.why_real: If the active shard is missing or incomplete without a sentinel/quarantine artifact, including after the interrupted-reindex tail-shard case, the system serves an incomplete active vector shard instead of scheduling repair. That contradicts the completeness-oracle invariant and silently degrades rec
p1.suggested_fix: Treat a non-complete shard with expected successful rows as degraded even without a sentinel, and schedule a repair with a distinct reason such as incomplete shard without sentinel. Keep expected == 0 as the safe empty-database case.
p1.v2_proof: The shipped 020 contract defines exactly two repair triggers — sentinel resume and the orphan-quarantine backstop (020/spec.md:76-78, implementation-summary.md:63), with completeness used only to discriminate stale sentinels from unfinished repairs — and handle_vector_shard_repair_assessment (vector-index-store.ts:677-691) implements precisely that documented set; marker-less detection was never claimed, and the cited tail-shard entry path is idx 8's defect, not this assessment's.

p1.title: Completeness check can clear repair intent when vector IDs are incomplete
p1.evidence: assess_vector_shard_repair_state treats a shard as complete when probe.ok and vecRows >= successful memory rows. It never verifies that every successful memory_index id exists in the vector table; adjacent mutation paths update active_vec.vec_memories only, not vec_<dim>, while active non-default vector queries join memory_index to vec_<dim> by id.
p1.why_real: A shard with equal counts but the wrong IDs, for example one stale extra vector row and one missing successful memory id, passes this oracle. The stale sentinel is then cleared at line 679 and no repair runs, leaving the missing successful memory unreturned by vector retrieval despite the repair-int
p1.suggested_fix: Replace the rowcount-only oracle with an ID-coverage check against the selected vector table and require zero successful memory rows missing a vector row. Add a regression fixture with equal counts but one missing successful id plus one stale extra vector id.
p1.v2_proof: vector-index-store.ts:641 (`vecRows >= expected`, no ID coverage) and the sentinel clear at 678-681 are real, but the equal-count/wrong-ID shard needed to trigger it arises mainly through idx 3's vec_memories-only mutations under a non-default embedder (deletes at vector-index-mutations.ts:675 never touch vec_<dim>); edge-case oracle weakness whose root cause is tracked at idx 3.
p1.p2_reason: Duplicate of idx 31; ID-based vector completeness oracle is too broad for P2.

p1.title: Strict-validation PASS claim relies on untracked metadata files
p1.evidence: The summary claims strict spec validation passed, but `git cat-file -e HEAD:<phase>/description.json` and `git cat-file -e HEAD:<phase>/graph-metadata.json` both report the paths exist on disk but not in HEAD. The committed phase tree contains spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, implementation-summary.md, and review artifacts, but not those metadata files.
p1.why_real: The user asked for the current committed state. The validation claim is a shipped truth claim, but the committed phase is missing required metadata files; a clean checkout would not match the working-tree validation state.
p1.suggested_fix: Commit the generated `description.json` and `graph-metadata.json` for this phase, or revise the completion/validation claims until committed-state validation is actually green.
p1.v2_proof: git status shows 021-hybrid-search-scope-then-limit/description.json and graph-metadata.json as untracked (??) and git cat-file -e confirms neither exists in HEAD, while sibling phases 005/011/015 all have description.json committed and the committed implementation-summary.md:107 claims 'validate.sh ... --strict | PASS, exit 0' — the committed phase tree cannot reproduce its own shipped completion evidence and is invisible to memory/graph tooling from a clean checkout.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: Commit the working-tree description.json and graph-metadata.json for the 021 phase (scoped git commit --only) so the committed tree matches the shipped validation claim.

p1.title: filePath fallback heuristic classifies human saves as 'import', stripping manual-overwrite protection from rows in index/scan-named spec paths
p1.evidence: deriveSourceKindFromContext falls back to `/\b(import|ingest|scan|index)\b/i.test(filePath)` -> 'import' (write-provenance.ts:89-93). memory-save.ts:2271-2276 always includes `filePath: routedFilePath` in writeProvenance, and pe-gating.ts:228/344 merge `filePath: parsed.filePath`; for a plain human memory_save (no provenanceSource/actor, tool='memory_save' matches no label, empty scope) the path heuristic is the deciding rule. Verified against real repo paths: `.../003-memory-index-causal-lifecy
p1.why_real: The genuine import paths never need this heuristic: scan sets provenanceSource 'memory_index_scan' explicitly (memory-save.ts:2857-2864) and async ingest sets defaults at its persist site, so the only writes the filePath branch actually decides are untagged (human-default) saves whose document path
p1.suggested_fix: Remove the filePath branch from deriveSourceKindFromContext (or restrict it to call sites whose origin is genuinely scan/ingest, where explicit provenanceSource already exists), so an untagged write truly defaults to 'human' as the changelog claims; add a regression test that an empty/path-only cont
p1.v2_proof: deriveSourceKindFromContext no longer tests filePath at all — write-provenance.ts:90-94 carries an explicit comment ('No filePath heuristic: a human save whose path merely contains a word like "index" or "scan" must not be classified as an automated import'), exactly the fix this finding proposed.

The changelog meanwhile asserts 'Provenance

This is a truthfulness defect in shipped docs even tho
p1.suggested_fix: Fill the implementation-summary verification table and checklist evidence rows with the actual command results (tsc exit 0, vitest pass counts, validate.sh output, hygiene check), or downgrade Status from Complete until they are recorded.

p1.title: Changelog materially overstates guard enforcement
p1.evidence: The changelog says provenance injection means the write-ingress guard enforces the constitutional rule that automated writers never overwrite manual or constitutional memory, while the phase spec and implementation summary explicitly document same-path retire and auto-promotion guard gaps as follow-ons.
p1.why_real: This is a shipped-truthfulness issue: readers can conclude guard enforcement is complete when the phase itself says two P0-class guard-coverage gaps remain open.
p1.suggested_fix: Narrow the changelog claim to provenance tagging and covered guard paths, and explicitly state that same-path retire and auto-promotion enforcement remain follow-on work.

p1.title: Caller-supplied provenance labels are persisted without secret scrubbing
p1.evidence: WriteProvenanceContext accepts provenanceSource/provenanceActor as unknown caller input, then buildPostInsertProvenanceFields normalizes those strings and returns them verbatim for persistence; persistProvenanceMetadata writes those fields into memory_index.
p1.why_real: A caller can accidentally or maliciously place credentials in provenanceSource/provenanceActor, and the committed helper stores them directly in durable metadata columns. That is a real security/data-retention bug in the provenance write path.
p1.suggested_fix: Scrub normalized provenance_source and provenance_actor before returning them from buildPostInsertProvenanceFields, and add a regression test that secret-like provenance labels are redacted before persistence.
p1.v2_proof: buildPostInsertProvenanceFields routes both labels through scrubSecrets (write-provenance.ts:108-110 scrubOptional, :116-123 applied to provenanceSource/provenanceActor) with an in-code comment stating they 'pass through the same fail-closed scrubber as content'.

p1.title: Completion is claimed while required final verification is still pending
p1.evidence: The phase summary marks Status as Complete, but the same file still says final TypeScript, provenance/guard suite, strict validation, and comment-hygiene checks are Pending final run at lines 118-121; line 16 also says the next safe action is to record those final results after they run.
p1.why_real: The shipped docs present completion/release-readiness while their own verification table says required handoff checks have not run. That is a materially false shipped claim, independent of code behavior.
p1.suggested_fix: Either run and record the final verification evidence, or downgrade the phase status/completion metadata until those checks are actually complete.
p1.v2_proof: The cited 'Pending final run' rows no longer exist — 022 implementation-summary.md's Verification table now lists PASS results with exit codes and counts for all five checks (rerun at epic review close, 2026-06-11).

p1.title: PE reinforce can strip manual-row protection by overwriting source_kind
p1.evidence: reinforceExistingMemory applies caller provenance directly to the existing memoryId after updating the row. The new test seeds the row as human and asserts the reinforced row becomes source_kind: 'system' at tests/pe-gating-provenance.vitest.ts:188-205.
p1.why_real: source_kind is also the protection signal: memory-crud-update treats human/null rows as protected and deliberately preserves source_kind='human' for automated updates while only recording provenance metadata. Flipping a human row to agent/system during a harmless PE reinforce makes later automated p
p1.suggested_fix: Do not overwrite source_kind for protected existing rows during reinforce; preserve human/null/constitutional protection while persisting provenance_source/provenance_actor, or add a separate last-writer provenance field. Add a regression that a human row reinforced by an automated save remains prot
p1.v2_proof: pe-gating.ts:228-232 calls applyWriteProvenance which unconditionally persists the newly derived source_kind (write-provenance.ts:162-171) with no protected-row check; tests/pe-gating-provenance.vitest.ts seeds source_kind 'human' (:42-63) and asserts the reinforced row becomes 'system' (:188-210), even though reinforce COALESCEs content_text (pe-gating.ts:216) so the row still holds the human-authored content — contradicting both constitutional/automated-writers-never-overwrite-manual.md ('Once a human edit lands, the memory row becomes protected from future automated overwrites') and memory-crud-update.ts:130-132, which deliberately preserves the existing source_kind for protected rows.
p1.v2_fix_sketch: In PE reinforce/update paths, preserve source_kind for human/null/constitutional rows (mirroring crud-update's storedSourceKind logic) while still persisting provenance_source/provenance_actor, and re-pin the test to assert preservation.

p1.title: implementation-summary.md documents the pre-remediation state, not what shipped
p1.evidence: Verification table claims 'Flag-on idempotency/near-duplicate suite after fixes | PASS ... 10 tests passed', but the shipped test file contains 12 tests (memory-idempotency-and-near-duplicate.vitest.ts has 12 it() blocks, including 'signals won/lost on store so a concurrent loser replays the winner by key' at line 122). The What Was Built section (lines 53-72) lists only three fixes and never mentions the shipped concurrent-loser replay path (memory-save.ts:3607-3618), the new lookupIdempotencyR
p1.why_real: The implementation-summary is the canonical completion record (the resume ladder reads it first), its continuity block says 'Phase complete; preserve final verification evidence', yet its verification evidence describes an earlier code state. The two shipped records conflict on what shipped and what
p1.suggested_fix: Regenerate/patch implementation-summary.md to reflect the post-remediation state: 12/12 test result, the lookupIdempotencyReceiptByKey addition, the boolean store contract, and the concurrent-loser replay behavior in memory-save.ts.

p1.title: Lost receipt-store conflicts fall through to a successful divergent response
p1.evidence: `lookupIdempotencyReceiptByKey` can return `conflict` when the stored payload hash differs (`idempotency-receipts.ts` lines 136-138), but the lost-store path only handles `winner.status === 'replay'` at lines 3615-3618 and otherwise falls through to `return response` at line 3625.
p1.why_real: Concurrent same-key requests with a genuine payload difference can both miss before storage; the loser can mutate, lose receipt insertion, detect a conflict by key, and still return its own success response instead of the documented idempotency conflict.
p1.suggested_fix: Handle the post-store `conflict` branch explicitly by returning the same `idempotency_key_conflict` error as the pre-save lookup path, and pair it with pre-mutation reservation or rollback so the conflicting mutation is not committed.
p1.p2_reason: Receipt conflict handling needs reservation/rollback design and is too risky for P2.

p1.title: Idempotency receipt is created only after the mutating save has already run
p1.evidence: The handler performs the receipt lookup before the save at lines 3445-3468, then runs `indexMemoryFile` at lines 3521-3528, and only stores the receipt after building the response at lines 3607-3610.
p1.why_real: Two concurrent same-key requests can both observe a miss before either receipt exists, then both proceed into the mutating save path. The later `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` only protects the receipt table and can at most change the loser's returned response; it does not prevent or roll back the loser's
p1.suggested_fix: Reserve the idempotency key before the mutating save, or move the miss/check/store decision under the same lock/transaction so only one request can execute the mutation and losers wait/replay before writing.
p1.v2_proof: memory-save.ts:3445-3468/3608-3619 implements a deliberate optimistic design documented in 023's implementation-summary.md:59-63 and spec.md REQ/NFR-R02 ('retry after failure is not blocked' — pre-reserving the key would block retries after failed saves, which the phase explicitly rejected); the concurrent loser's 'mutation' is a same-content re-index that dedupes to unchanged/duplicate with no integrity effect, and the whole path is default-off (idempotency-receipts.ts:54-57).

p1.title: Flag-off suite verification is overstated because the test file forces the flag on
p1.evidence: The summary claims a flag-off idempotency/near-duplicate suite passed, and spec.md requires running the test file with the flag enabled and unset. But the test file's beforeEach unconditionally sets process.env.SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY = 'true' at memory-idempotency-and-near-duplicate.vitest.ts:66-68, so an externally unset flag run would still execute most tests as flag-on.
p1.why_real: The shipped verification evidence implies full default-off regression coverage, but code reality only has targeted assertions for isMemoryIdempotencyEnabled default behavior and one near-duplicate flag-off case. That materially overstates the completed handoff criteria.
p1.suggested_fix: Either add a real flag-off describe/parameterized run that does not force the env var on and covers the save path, or narrow the implementation summary and checklist language to the specific default-helper and near-duplicate assertions that actually exist.
p1.v2_proof: memory-idempotency-and-near-duplicate.vitest.ts:66-68 confirms beforeEach unconditionally sets SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY='true', so the 'Flag-off suite PASS' row (implementation-summary.md:106) ran most tests flag-on with genuine flag-off coverage limited to the default-helper test (:146-157) and one near-duplicate case (:314-340) — but spec.md:144 REQ-007's literal acceptance criterion ('test file passes with the flag unset') was met and the summary records runs that actually happened, so this is a verification-coverage precision gap, not a materially false shipped doc.
p1.p2_reason: Spec-folder verification language edits were explicitly out of scope.

p1.title: Concurrent same-key memory_save losers can still return non-idempotent duplicate/unchanged responses
p1.evidence: The handler does the receipt lookup before indexing at lines 3445-3468, but after indexing it only enters the lost-store replay path when shouldStoreMemorySaveReceipt(result, response) is true at line 3608. That guard only allows indexed/updated/deferred results in idempotency-receipts.ts:104-113, so a concurrent identical request that missed the pre-lookup but then hits the normal duplicate/unchanged dedup path never re-checks the winner receipt and returns the duplicate/unchanged response inst
p1.why_real: This violates the phase claim that a concurrent first-write loser replays the winner and breaks response-stable idempotency when SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY is enabled. The added test exercises storeIdempotencyReceipt directly, but not the actual handleMemorySave path where duplicate/unchanged losers
p1.suggested_fix: Keep the idempotency lookup, mutation, and receipt store/replay decision in one critical section or add a post-save receipt re-check for same-key duplicate/unchanged losers. Also handle a post-store conflict result explicitly instead of falling through to the loser response.
p1.v2_proof: memory-save.ts:3608 gates the lost-race replay on shouldStoreMemorySaveReceipt whose allowlist (idempotency-receipts.ts:41,104-113) excludes duplicate/unchanged, so a concurrent loser that deduped against the winner returns its own divergent response — contradicting the in-code intent comment at memory-save.ts:3611-3614 ('instead of its own divergent (e.g. unchanged) outcome') — but SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY is default-off (idempotency-receipts.ts:54-57) and the data state converges, so it is a real flag-on response-stability gap, not a today-default P1.
p1.p2_reason: Concurrent same-key idempotency critical-section changes are too risky for P2.
